title: MCP blocking-verb fence omits monitor-flow / verify-canary / submit-flow: one tool call wedges the synchronous server for up to 24h, and the default in-process runner has no deadline at all
failure_scenario: User runs `hpc-agent mcp-serve --allow-mutations` (full or tiered catalog — both expose every verb='workflow' primitive per _MUTATING_VERBS at mcp_server.py:135/1167, and tiered's run-primitive advertises 'any registered'). Agent calls monitor-flow {spec:{run_id:...}} to watch a live array mid-campaign. monitor-flow is a poll-to-terminal loop (default wall_clock_budget_seconds=86400) and is NOT in _DETACH_REQUIRED_VERBS (:965-975) or _BLOCKING_WAIT_VERBS (:988) — the exact proving-run-3 head-of-line class the seam was built for. Dispatch is single-threaded, so every later tool call (status reads, append-decision, doctor) queues behind it for up to 24h; an abandoned agent turn does not stop it. The 3600s cap (_SUBPROCESS_RUNNER_TIMEOUT_SEC, :834) only bounds the NON-default subprocess runner — the default _in_process_cli_runner (:882-946, chosen at :1308) has no timeout, so even the 1h backstop never fires. verify-canary (30-min default poll loop, wait-budget-sec raisable arbitrarily) and submit-flow's canary leg escape the same way. Worse: MonitorFlowSpec is extra='forbid' with no detach field, so there is no {detach:true} remedy — it is the wait-detached class (refuse outright) but is not refused.
fix_sketch: Add monitor-flow, verify-canary, and submit-flow to the seam in _refuse_blocking_over_mcp: monitor-flow and verify-canary join _BLOCKING_WAIT_VERBS-style outright refusal (no detach field exists in their specs; name status-watch{detach:true}/poll-detached and the S2 detached canary as the sanctioned alternatives), or grow a detach field like aggregate-flow did. Independently, give _in_process_cli_runner a bounded-wait posture (or apply _SUBPROCESS_RUNNER_TIMEOUT_SEC uniformly) so the wedge class cannot recur through the default runner as the comment at :829-834 already claims it cannot.
evidence: Read _DETACH_REQUIRED_VERBS/_BLOCKING_WAIT_VERBS/_refuse_blocking_over_mcp and both runners at d731b12; confirmed monitor-flow/verify-canary/submit-flow are verb='workflow' agent_facing in operations.json (reachable via allowed_primitives with allow_mutations); confirmed monitor_flow's sleep-and-repeat loop and the 86400s default; confirmed verify_canary's _DEFAULT_WAIT_BUDGET_SEC=1800 poll loop. tests/test_mcp_server.py:335-470 pins refusals only for submit-s2/s3/s4, status-watch, aggregate-run, aggregate-flow, campaign-run, wait-detached — using allow_mutations=True servers, proving the exposure path is exercised; no test covers monitor-flow/verify-canary/submit-flow. Not covered by docs/plans/architecture-review-2026-07-13.md (its MCP item is the _CURATED_EXTRA_VERBS hygiene note) nor the fixed 2026-07-11 sweep.
triage: Verified mcp_server.py:965-988 — _DETACH_REQUIRED_VERBS and _BLOCKING_WAIT_VERBS exclude monitor-flow/verify-canary/submit-flow; head-of-line wedge class real, not in banked MCP hygiene item.

votes.reasoning: Reproduced in the venv: _refuse_blocking_over_mcp lets monitor-flow/verify-canary/submit-flow pass while refusing status-watch/aggregate-run/submit-s2; all three are verb='workflow' agent_facing and INVOCABLE under the default full and tiered catalogs with allow_mutations. monitor-flow is a poll loop defaulting to 86400s, verify-canary to 1800s; dispatch is single-threaded synchronous in-process and the default _in_process_cli_runner (mcp_server.py:1308) has no timeout, so the 3600s cap never applies. verify-canary is the documented direct step after the two-phase canary gate, making the wedge reachable in normal agent use.

Read the seam and both runners directly: monitor-flow (default 86400s poll loop, extra='forbid' spec with no detach field) and verify-canary (1800s poll loop) are verb='workflow' agent_facing primitives, so allowed_primitives exposes them under --allow-mutations, yet neither is in _DETACH_REQUIRED_VERBS nor _BLOCKING_WAIT_VERBS, and the default _in_process_cli_runner has no timeout (the 3600s cap is subprocess-only). A direct call to monitor-flow therefore wedges the single-threaded server for up to 24h with no {detach:true} remedy and no backstop — exactly the head-of-line class the seam was built to refuse, which status-watch (the detach-gated wrapper over this same poll) IS protected against.

title: A previous run's stale _combiner/wave_N.json is silently adopted as the current run's combined wave, and every reducer merges foreign wave partials without checking run_id
failure_scenario: Combiner output is not run-namespaced: every run writes `_combiner/wave_<N>.json` at the shared remote_path root, and PROTECTED_OUTPUT_DIRS (transport.py:107-109) deliberately shields `_combiner/` from every re-deploy's --delete, so run A's partials persist forever. User submits run B to the same remote_path (the normal deploy-once model). monitor-flow auto-combine / aggregate-flow's _combine_missing calls combine_wave with force=False on wave 0; the cluster combiner refuses with 'output already exists: _combiner/wave_0.json (use --force' because run A's file is there; combine.py:156-161's recovery regex reads that refusal as 'this wave is already combined' and journals wave 0 of run B as combined — run B's combiner never runs. aggregate-flow then pulls the whole `_combiner/` dir (first pull is unfiltered) and reduce_partials merges run A's grid_points as run B's aggregated_metrics; the same happens on the HPC_CLUSTER_FINAL_REDUCE=1 path because _final_reduce merges every wave_*.json and never compares data['run_id'] to the target run_id, so even a force-combined run B still absorbs run A's leftover higher-numbered waves. campaign_run.py:400 consumes these aggregated_metrics directly, so a multi-run campaign's optimizer steps on the previous run's numbers with zero error.
fix_sketch: Namespace the combiner output per run (`_combiner/<run_id>/wave_<N>.json`), or (minimal, back-compatible): (a) in the cluster combiner's no-force existing-output check, read the existing file's run_id and only refuse when it matches — overwrite (or refuse with a distinct 'foreign partial' message) when it differs; (b) make combine.py's _ALREADY_COMBINED_RE recovery verify the remote file's run_id before journaling the wave combined; (c) have reduce_partials/collect_wave_errors and _final_reduce skip (and disclose) any wave file whose run_id != the run being reduced.
evidence: Read combine.py (recovery at 133-161), combiner.py (out_path has no run_id; _final_reduce iterates _wave_partial_files with no run_id filter), metrics.py reduce_partials (merges data['grid_points'] unconditionally), transport.py run_combiner/run_final_reduce (cd remote_path; relative _combiner), PROTECTED_OUTPUT_DIRS, aggregate_flow._combine_missing (first attempt force=False) and monitor_flow.py:696-702 (same). tests/ops/aggregate/test_combine_wave_idempotent.py:97 pins the recovery only for the SAME run (journal lost); no test covers a foreign run's stale file. verify-aggregation-complete would flag provenance/unexpected_tasks, but it only runs on the separate aggregate-check surface — aggregate-flow/campaign-run return the contaminated metrics without it. Not in the 2026-07 fixed list or the refuted list (checked both).
triage: Cross-run _combiner contamination; _combiner/ confirmed in PROTECTED_OUTPUT_DIRS (transport.py:109) shielding stale files; reduce/final-reduce run_id filtering absent. Not in fixed/refuted.

votes.reasoning: Verified run_combiner cd's to remote_path and writes non-run-namespaced _combiner/wave_N.json; _combiner/ is PROTECTED_OUTPUT_DIRS so run A's file survives run B's --delete redeploy; the combiner's existence-refusal (before any task read) is matched by _ALREADY_COMBINED_RE and journaled as combined for run B; reduce_partials merges grid_points with no run_id check; campaign_run.py:400 consumes the metrics with no aggregate-check guard. reproduce_run.py:82 uses a distinct remote_path specifically to avoid this, confirming normal re-runs share it. Cross-run contamination is real and reachable on the campaign/re-run path.

Traced the full chain: remote_path defaults to scratch/run_name and is carried forward from latest_run, so re-submitting the same experiment (new run_id after a code edit) reuses the path; _combiner/ is in PROTECTED_OUTPUT_DIRS so run A's non-namespaced _combiner/wave_N.json survives run B's --delete; the combiner refuses no-force when the file exists (combiner.py:445) and combine.py:156 marks the wave combined via _ALREADY_COMBINED_RE without checking run_id; and both reduce_partials (metrics.py:242) and _final_reduce (combiner.py:276) merge every wave_*.json without filtering the top-level data['run_id'] the file actually carries. aggregate-flow's first _combine_missing attempt is force=False, so run B silently adopts run A's grid_points and feeds them to campaign optimizers.

title: Resubmitted (recovered) tasks' results never enter the aggregate: nothing invalidates combined_waves, so the stale partial wave file is served forever
failure_scenario: Wave 2 of a 500-task run finishes with 40 failed tasks; monitor-flow auto-combines it (combiner exits 0, records the 40 misses in the partial's errors list, journal marks wave 2 combined). User runs the documented recover/resubmit flow (resubmit_flow reuses the SAME run_id and only extends job_ids); the 40 tasks now complete and write metrics.json. User re-runs aggregate-flow: _missing_waves skips every wave already in record.combined_waves, monitor-flow's auto-combine seeds state.last_combined_waves from the record (monitor_flow.py:400) so it also skips, and the cluster's wave_2.json is never recombined — the recovered tasks' metrics are permanently excluded from aggregated_metrics (and any partial metrics.json a task wrote before dying stays averaged in). The only signal is escalation_reason 'partial_waves:metrics_unreadable...' which is now factually wrong (the metrics are readable), steering the user away from the real remediation. verify-aggregation-complete cannot catch it either (see the vacuous all_tasks_present finding). grep shows no writer of combined_waves outside combine.py's _apply — resubmit_flow never touches it.
fix_sketch: In resubmit_flow (and the auto-resume composite), after a successful cluster resubmit of failed_task_ids, map those task ids to waves via the sidecar wave_map and update_run_record to remove those waves from combined_waves (and reset monitor's combiner_attempts), so the next monitor/aggregate pass re-runs the combiner with force for exactly the affected waves. Pair with the incremental-pull invalidation fix so the refreshed partial actually reaches the local reduce.
evidence: Read recover_flow.resubmit_flow end-to-end (reuses run_id, updates job_ids/pending_resubmit only; zero references to combined_waves/failed_waves), aggregate_flow._missing_waves + the ensure_all_combined block, monitor_flow's auto-combine seeding. Grep for combined_waves writers: only ops/aggregate/combine.py. No test exercises resubmit-then-aggregate; escalation text at aggregate_flow.py:1739-1743 confirms the stale disclosure wording.
triage: Resubmitted tasks never invalidate combined_waves; novel, no recorded fix; combined_waves only written by combine.py per finding. Real result-exclusion.

related.note: Compounding: F07 (verify-aggregation-complete's all_tasks_present is a tautology) is exactly why F06 (resubmitted/recovered tasks silently excluded from the aggregate) goes undetected; F06 explicitly cross-references F07. Distinct root causes (resubmit doesn't invalidate combined_waves vs combiner echoes full task_ids).

votes.reasoning: recover_flow.resubmit_flow reuses run_id and never writes combined_waves; the only writers are combine.py._apply and reconcile.py (which derives from existing wave files, keeping the stale wave combined). _missing_waves and monitor auto-combine both skip a wave already in combined_waves, so the recovered tasks' metrics are permanently excluded. One nuance: the stale wave's residual errors list fires a misleading escalation_reason (so not literally silent), but the finding acknowledges this and the exclusion is real and unrecoverable via the documented recover->re-aggregate loop (even the manual --force fix is defeated by F08's local filename-pin).

Grep confirms combined_waves is only added-to by combine.py:170 and re-derived by reconcile via file existence (ls _combiner/wave_*.json), which keeps a stale wave file as 'combined'; combine.py:133 refuses to re-run an already-combined wave without force; resubmit_flow reuses run_id and extends job_ids but never touches combined_waves. So recovered tasks' metrics are permanently excluded from the aggregate with a misleading escalation reason. Reachable via the documented recover-then-aggregate flow.

title: verify-aggregation-complete's all_tasks_present is a tautology: the combiner echoes the full wave_map task list into task_ids even for tasks whose metrics were never read, so the integrity gate green-lights aggregates computed over a subset
failure_scenario: A wave of 100 tasks combines while 40 tasks never wrote metrics.json (or NFS hadn't surfaced them): the cluster combiner appends 40 entries to the partial's `errors` list, aggregates only 60 tasks into grid_points — but writes `"task_ids": list(task_ids)` (the full wave_map slice, combiner.py:549). verify_aggregation_complete computes pulled_tasks from that task_ids field and never reads the partial's `errors`, so missing_tasks=[] and ok=True. The aggregate-check block's missing_tasks decision point ('some tasks never wrote metrics into any wave partial; the aggregate would be computed over a subset', aggregate_blocks.py:136-141) describes exactly this state yet can never fire for a production-written partial — it only fires for whole missing wave FILES. A user who runs the trust-the-numbers gate before a greenlight gets 'every task accounted for' on a mean computed over 60% of tasks; the only disclosure lives on a different surface (aggregate-flow's escalation_reason).
fix_sketch: Make the invariant read the evidence the partial already carries: in verify_aggregation_complete, parse each pulled wave doc's `errors` list (entries are formatted 'task {tid}: ...'; or better, have the combiner additionally emit a `tasks_read` list) and subtract errored/unread task ids from pulled_tasks so they surface as missing_tasks. Add a test seeding a partial with full task_ids + non-empty errors asserting ok=False.
evidence: Read combiner.py main() (task_ids from wave_map at line 400, errors appended at 493-494, output at 546-552) and invariants.py (pulled_tasks from doc['task_ids'] only; grep shows nothing in invariants.py reads doc['errors']). tests/ops/aggregate/test_invariants.py's fixture hard-codes errors:[] and only exercises missing_tasks via absent wave files or hand-truncated task_ids the production combiner never emits — the real-world arm is unpinned. Distinct from the fixed implicit-wave-0 bug (bug-sweep DISPUTED entry) which was about empty wave_maps, not errored tasks.
triage: Verified combiner.py:549 writes full list(task_ids) while errors are separate; invariants.py:346 computes pulled_tasks from task_ids only, never reads doc['errors'] — vacuous gate confirmed. Distinct from the fixed implicit-wave-0 DISPUTED entry.

related.note: Compounding: F07 (verify-aggregation-complete's all_tasks_present is a tautology) is exactly why F06 (resubmitted/recovered tasks silently excluded from the aggregate) goes undetected; F06 explicitly cross-references F07. Distinct root causes (resubmit doesn't invalidate combined_waves vs combiner echoes full task_ids).

votes.reasoning: Read combiner.py:549 (task_ids = full list(task_ids) from wave_map) with failed tasks appended only to errors (551), and invariants.py:340-354 which builds pulled_tasks from doc['task_ids'] alone and never reads doc['errors']; missing_tasks is therefore [] and all_tasks_present True for any production partial with errored tasks, so the aggregate_blocks.py:132 missing_tasks decision point can only fire for whole-missing wave FILES. Vacuous completeness gate confirmed; a sibling escalation_reason on aggregate-flow partially mitigates but the integrity gate itself always passes.

combiner.py:549 writes list(task_ids) = the full wave_map slice regardless of which tasks errored (errors go to a separate list, :464-494); invariants.py:346 derives pulled_tasks from doc['task_ids'] only and grep confirms it never reads doc['errors'], so missing_tasks is always empty for present waves and all_tasks_present is a tautology. The test fixture hard-codes errors:[] (test_invariants.py:52) and only exercises the whole-missing-file arm, leaving the production-errored-task case unpinned.

title: Campaign overnight 'auto-advance' of an anomaly halt is a no-op that masks the halt: consuming stop_circuit_breaker/stop_resubmit_cap/stop_over_budget cannot make the campaign continue, yet reports watching_healthy ('self-chaining continues') all night
failure_scenario: Async campaign with a live campaign-scope standing consent trips the circuit breaker (or budget halt) at 1am. campaign_watch consults consume_boundary_under_consent, gets consumed=True, and returns stage_reached='watching_healthy', needs_decision=False, reason 'auto-advanced ... self-chaining continues' — with NO next_block. But nothing clears the underlying condition: campaign-advance recomputes the same stop_* every tick (consecutive terminal failures never reset because no new run is ever submitted; stop_over_budget needs a budget ack that is never recorded), and the ladder puts stop_* BEFORE _refill, so campaign-refill returns no_refill_needed forever. The campaign is dead all night while every watch tick reports healthy; the human boundary (anomaly_brief y/nudge) is suppressed for as long as the consent is live. Worse, _already_consumed keys on (consumed_block='campaign-watch', detail.cmd_sha=campaign identity), so a SECOND, DIFFERENT anomaly the same night (breaker then over-budget) is consumed with line=None — masked with zero ledger disclosure. The human wakes to a morning brief claiming the anomaly auto-advanced and the campaign continued, when in fact it wedged at the first anomaly.
fix_sketch: Either (a) make consumption actually enable continuation: on a consumed stop_over_budget, snapshot a budget ack (ack_covers_spend mechanism, meta/campaign/budget_ack.py) bounded by the consent's budget_cap, and for breaker/resubmit-cap either park regardless (they are diagnostic halts a consent should not override) or record a consent-scoped override token campaign_advance's rules consult; or (b) stop claiming continuation: return watching_anomaly with needs_decision=False + an explicit 'halted-under-consent, resumes at morning boundary' stage, and key _already_consumed on (consumed_block, anomaly decision, cmd_sha) so distinct anomalies each get a ledger line. Add a test driving campaign_watch consumed → campaign_refill and asserting the pool actually refills (today it cannot).
evidence: Read campaign_watch's consumed branch (returns watching_healthy, no next_block, blocks.py:448-459), campaign_advance's rules ladder (stop rules ordered before _refill, advance.py:406-409; breaker counts consecutive terminal failures which only a new successful run resets), campaign_refill (re-derives advance itself, returns no_refill_needed on any non-refill decision, campaign_refill.py:364-379), and _already_consumed (overnight.py:834-839). grep confirms nothing writes a budget ack or breaker reset on the consent path. tests/ops/decision/test_overnight_wiring.py:451-468 pins only the surface (watching_healthy + one ledger line) — the downstream 'nothing continues' consequence is untested.
triage: Overnight consume-masks-halt for stop_circuit_breaker/over_budget; novel campaign-overnight defect not covered by G3/RULING 3 (which is bound-capture, not the continuation no-op). Real overnight wedge.

votes.reasoning: Read blocks.py:417-459 (consume returns watching_healthy/'self-chaining continues', no next_block), advance.py:406-410 (stop_* rules ordered before _refill so refill can't fire while a halt holds), advance.py:284/299 (over-budget needs an ack, breaker needs a fresh success — neither is written on the consume path). Consuming the anomaly is a pure no-op mask: the same stop_* recomputes every reconcile tick, and _already_consumed's coarse (block,cmd_sha) key means a second/later anomaly logs line=None. The wiring test pins only the healthy surface; the downstream wedge and misleading morning brief are real and untested.

Verified every link: campaign-watch is a consumable boundary; consume_boundary_under_consent only writes an audit line (write_budget_ack is only called by the standalone campaign_acknowledge_budget atom, never on the watch path; nothing resets the breaker); the async ladder orders stop_* before _refill and the breaker resets only on a complete iteration, so the halt recomputes every tick while campaign_watch reports watching_healthy; _already_consumed keys on (block,cmd_sha) not the anomaly kind, masking a second distinct anomaly with line=None. Real overnight wedge.

title: Driver and Stop guard disagree on 'y then a later record': the driver scans past a retraction nudge and consumes the retracted greenlight (launching the un-redrafted spec), while the guard goes silent on any unrelated later record (bedtime consent, sign-off), stalling a genuinely committed y
failure_scenario: Direction (a), driver side: human answers the S3 brief 'y' (targets submit-s3, ts > awaiting_since), then seconds later nudges 'wait — raise the walltime first' and goes to bed expecting a redraft. The stop guard correctly stays silent (test_y_then_nudge_latest_wins_is_silent pins 'latest wins → still waiting'), but the 2am watch-terminal/doctor tick calls _boundary_scoped_committed_resolved, which scans NEWEST-FIRST and skips the nudge (response!='y') to return the retracted y → routes advance → submit-s3's gate also passes (latest greenlight names it) → the full main array launches with the pre-nudge spec. Direction (b), guard side: human commits the targeting y and the agent journals the overnight-consent record (or any other same-scope touchpoint) right after it in the same turn; find_committed_unadvanced tests ONLY decisions[-1], which is now the consent record → guard silent → turn ends with the y committed-but-unadvanced, the exact 2026-06-10 stall the guard exists for, until the doctor backstop. block_drive.py:1036-1039 claims the shared predicate makes the two surfaces unable to drift — but they apply it to different record sets.
fix_sketch: Unify the semantics: in _boundary_scoped_committed_resolved, stop the newest-first scan at the first SAME-BOUNDARY record of either kind — a nudge that answers this parked boundary (ts >= awaiting_since) supersedes an earlier y (return None → awaiting_decision); in find_committed_unadvanced, scan newest-first with the same rule instead of testing only decisions[-1], so an unrelated later record (overnight-consent, sign-off, another block's touchpoint) neither silences the guard nor blocks the driver. Add paired tests: y-then-same-boundary-nudge → driver awaits; y-then-overnight-consent → guard forces continue.
evidence: Read both call sites: the driver loops `for record in reversed(records)` returning the first targeting y (block_drive.py:1101-1105); the guard checks `decisions[-1]` only (stop_guard.py:151-155). tests/_kernel/hooks/test_decision_rendezvous_hooks.py:145 (test_y_then_nudge_latest_wins_is_silent) pins the guard's 'latest wins' semantic; no test in tests/_kernel/lifecycle/test_block_drive.py covers y-then-nudge at the same boundary (grep 'nudge' shows only field-edit routing tests), so the driver's consume-the-retracted-y behavior is unpinned. block_gate's superseded-consumption backstops (block_gate.py:109-120) do not cover this: the retracted y IS the latest greenlight naming the verb, and no terminal exists yet to replay.
triage: Verified block_drive.py:1101 driver scans reversed(records) returning first targeting-y (skips trailing nudge) while stop_guard.py:151 checks decisions[-1] only — genuine asymmetry. Distinct from the boundary-scoping fix for #1/#23 (which is present); this is the same-boundary retraction / unrelated-later-record edge.

votes.reasoning: Verified the asymmetry directly: driver _boundary_scoped_committed_resolved (1101-1105) loops reversed(records) returning the first greenlight_targets_boundary match, skipping a trailing nudge and consuming the retracted y (ts>=awaiting_since passes); assert_greenlit_target (block_gate.py:123-127) passes it too, so submit-s3 launches the pre-nudge spec. The guard checks only decisions[-1] (stop_guard.py:151-156) and stays silent. Nudges are journaled decision records (test _commit_nudge), so y-then-nudge is realizable and unpinned in test_block_drive; the shared-predicate 'cannot drift' claim is applied to different record sets.

Read both call sites: the driver's _boundary_scoped_committed_resolved (block_drive.py:1101) loops `for record in reversed(records)` and returns the first record where greenlight_targets_boundary holds — a trailing nudge (response!='y') is skipped, so an earlier boundary-targeting 'y' is consumed and routed to advance; the Stop guard (stop_guard.py:151) tests only decisions[-1], so a trailing nudge/consent silences it (latest-wins). A bare nudge commit does not bump awaiting_since or re-park, so the 'y' stays valid (ts>=awaiting_since) and a later detached/cron tick advances it. The docstring at block_drive.py:1036 explicitly claims the two surfaces 'cannot drift'; the reversed-scan vs decisions[-1] asymmetry falsifies that and is unpinned by tests (only the guard's y-then-nudge case is pinned). Direction (a)'s severe outcome is reachable but conditional (nudge journaled, no re-park, automated tick); direction (b) is easily reachable but milder.

title: Kill-type task deaths never announce, and the announce-census path never falls back to the scheduler — monitor wedges at 'pending' until budget, auto-resume/auto-recover unreachable
failure_scenario: Multi-day array on a preemptable SLURM/SGE queue. Tasks start finishing, so the dispatcher creates .hpc/announce/<run_id>/ and monitor_flow._announce_status switches the poll loop to census-only ('present' => NO reporter walk, ever). Then the scheduler bumps 10 tasks: the dispatcher's preemption handler marks the sidecar and sys.exit(130) WITHOUT writing a terminal marker (only the normal main-flow exit at dispatch.py:1328 announces; same for whole-job SIGKILL on hard walltime and node crashes). Those tasks are 'missing' in every future census, mapped to pending>0, so classify_polling never returns FAILED (needs pending==0) and never escalates unknown (census sets unknown=0). The scheduler's PREEMPTED/exit-130 evidence — the exact signal report_status folds into preempted_task_ids — is never queried again, so maybe_auto_resume (#299) and resolve-and-recover (both fired only under terminal==FAILED, monitor_flow.py:785+) never run. The watch rides the entire multi-hour wall-clock budget and returns TIMEOUT ('cap-overrun'), the bumped tasks are never resubmitted, and the campaign stalls until a human notices.
fix_sketch: (a) In dispatch.py's preemption handler, best-effort _write_announcement(state='failed' (or a new 'preempted' state), exit_code=130) before sys.exit — everything the handler needs (here/run_id/task_id) is in scope via the closure; also announce on the idempotency-skip exit (dispatch.py:920) so a marker-less completed task can re-announce on resubmit. (b) In _announce_status, when the census has been partial AND static for N ticks (or missing>0 with an empty scheduler queue — one cheap query_jobs call), fall through to the reporter walk instead of returning the census, so scheduler-terminal failure evidence (OOM/TIMEOUT/NODE_FAIL/PREEMPTED) reaches classify_polling and the auto-resume gate.
evidence: Read dispatch.py handler (no announcement call anywhere in _handler; announcement only after child.wait in main), monitor_flow census branch (walk only when census not 'present'), classify_polling arms, auto-resume wiring (monitor_flow.py:794-841 — only under LifecycleState.FAILED), and auto_resume_flow.py (sources preempted ids from scheduler exit codes 130/143 — evidence the census never carries). tests/ops/monitor/test_flow_announce.py:190 pins only the mechanism ('partial census rides to budget → TIMEOUT, no walk') — no test covers a task class that can NEVER announce (preempt/SIGKILL), which converts that pinned behavior into a permanent wedge. Not in the refuted list, upstream-fixes plan, or architecture review (which only mentions announce constants dedup).
triage: Verified dispatch.py:292-352 SIGTERM handler marks sidecar then sys.exit(_EXIT_PREEMPTED) with NO announcement write; census wedge for preempt/SIGKILL tasks. G1 Phase 2 was consumption end-to-end, not this producer gap.
merge_rationale: Same root cause: once the announce dir exists (present=1) the census leg never re-consults the scheduler/walk, so any task that dies without writing a terminal marker (preempt/SIGKILL/node-crash/OOM) stays missing->pending forever and neither the FAILED arm (pending==0) nor the unknown-escalation arm (unknown>0, hard-coded 0) can fire, riding the watch to TIMEOUT; F17 is stronger because it also covers the writer side (dispatcher not announcing on preemption) and the downstream auto-resume/auto-recover unreachability.

related.note: Same announce-census-no-scheduler-input seam, opposite triggers: F17/F24 = no marker so run never settles; F23 = stale .failed markers after a resubmit so a LIVE re-run is falsely settled FAILED and thrashes; F17's fix (announce/clear markers on resubmit) overlaps F23's remedy.

votes.reasoning: Read dispatch.py's SIGTERM handler (292-352): it marks the sidecar and sys.exit(130) with no _write_announcement — the only announce is at 1328 after child.wait. Once any task announces, read_announcements returns present=1 and monitor_flow's census leg (439-458) stops walking; _announce_status maps missing->pending, and classify_polling needs pending==0 for FAILED and hard-codes unknown=0, so walltime-kill/node-crash/preempt-no-requeue tasks stay missing->pending forever -> TIMEOUT, and auto-resume/recover (gated on FAILED at 785+) never fire. Every link real and reachable on a real array campaign.

The SIGTERM preemption handler (dispatch.py:292-352) marks the sidecar and sys.exit(_EXIT_PREEMPTED=130) with no _write_announcement call — announcements fire only in the main flow at dispatch.py:1328. Once any marker exists, monitor's census leg (monitor_flow.py:441) is taken every tick with no walk; a preempted/SIGKILL/node-crash task stays missing->pending, and classify_polling's FAILED arm needs pending==0 while the unknown-escalation arm needs unknown>0 (census hardcodes unknown=0) — so neither fires and the watch rides to TIMEOUT, leaving maybe_auto_resume (gated on FAILED) unreachable. Fully reachable on preemptable SLURM/SGE queues the dispatcher itself handles.

title: Cluster-shipped 'stdlib-only, any python3' files require Python 3.10/3.9 features: combiner's zip(strict=True) crashes every wave combine and the final reduce; metrics_io.removeprefix breaks read_kw_env on <=3.8 envs
failure_scenario: The combiner is scp'd standalone and run on the login node as `python3 _hpc_combiner.py` under the user's experiment env (or bare login python — RHEL/Rocky 8 ships python3 = 3.6.8; torch-1.x conda envs commonly pin 3.8/3.9). `zip(entries, weights, strict=True)` in _weighted_mean (combiner.py:153) is a TypeError ('zip() takes no keyword arguments') on every Python < 3.10 — so after a full campaign runs cleanly and burns its cluster hours, EVERY per-wave combine and the --final cross-wave reduce die with an uncaught traceback; monitor_flow exhausts combiner_max_retries, aggregate_flow._combine_missing fails identically, and the harvest is wedged with results stranded cluster-side. Separately, metrics_io ships as an importable stub for user executors on compute nodes; read_kw_env's str.removeprefix (metrics_io.py:44) AttributeErrors on <=3.8, failing every task that reads its params through the documented helper (the 1-task canary at least catches this one before the full array).
fix_sketch: combiner: drop strict=True (entries and weights are equal-length by construction — weights is built as a comprehension over entries) or add an explicit len check; metrics_io: use k[len(_KW_PREFIX):] instead of removeprefix. Then add a feature-floor gate to the AST scan that already enforces stdlib-only on _build_deploy_items files (e.g. run vermin, or compile() each shipped file with ast feature checks pinned to the documented cluster floor — dispatch.py's time.time_ns already sets it at 3.7), so ruff-style modernization (B905 added strict=True in commit c8c7854 — the fix train itself introduced this regression) can't silently raise the floor of deployed files again.
evidence: Read combiner.py (docstring: 'Stays zero-dependency — only Python stdlib'), transport.py run_combiner/run_final_reduce command strings, transport.py:527 and :1714 ('runs under any cluster python3', 'under any python'), pyproject requires-python >=3.10 (CI never executes shipped files under an older interpreter), tests/contracts/test_cluster_runtime_self_contained.py (imports under `python -S` but the CI python, so no version pin), and ops/preflight/check.py (no cluster python-version probe). git log -S shows strict=True introduced in c8c7854 (2026-07-11). No test or lint pins a version floor for deployed files.
triage: Verified combiner.py:153 zip(...,strict=True) (3.10+) and metrics_io.py:44 removeprefix (3.9+) both present in 'stdlib-only, any python3' shipped files. Not a G12 member; genuine version-floor regression, still live on HEAD.

title: Stale announce markers after a resubmit make the census classify a LIVE re-run as FAILED: auto-resume/auto-recover thrash duplicate arrays onto the cluster, then the run is marked terminal-FAILED while attempt 2 is still running
failure_scenario: Array run: 20 tasks, 3 announce `task_N.failed` markers on disk (dispatch.py writes one marker per task at terminal and only flips failed->complete when the NEW attempt itself terminates, dispatch.py:210-218). Any resubmit of those 3 tasks — auto-resume on preemption, auto-recover on OOM, or a manual /recover-hpc followed by re-watching — leaves the 3 `.failed` markers in place while attempt 2 sits queued. The monitor's announce-first leg (monitor_flow.py:439 runs _announce_status EVERY tick, never consulting the scheduler) then sees complete+failed==total, missing=0 -> classify_polling returns FAILED on the very next ~10-60s tick. With auto_resume_on_kill: resume #1 fires, next tick FAILED again (markers unchanged, fetch_failures re-reads attempt-1 stderr), resume #2 fires — now the SAME task ids run as two concurrent scheduler arrays writing the same result dirs — cap (max_auto_resumes=2) is burned in ~2 poll intervals, then mark_terminal(FAILED) while both re-run arrays are live and unwatched. Without any opt-in: user resubmits failed tasks, re-invokes monitor-flow, and the FIRST tick marks the run terminal FAILED. reconcile has the same hole: its announce fast path (reconcile.py:771-792) settles FAILED from the full-but-stale announcement BEFORE the scheduler alive-probe (submitted at :820) ever runs.
fix_sketch: On any resubmit (resubmit_flow, both auto composites), delete/rename the announce markers for the resubmitted task ids in the same remote exec that submits (one `rm .hpc/announce/<run_id>/task_{ids}.failed`), or record a per-run announce generation/epoch that the census compares against. Cheaper interim guard: in monitor_flow's census leg, when record.job_ids grew since the markers' verdict (or last tick took a resubmit action), force the walk/scheduler-alive check before accepting a failed-census terminal; in reconcile, run the alive-check before letting a full announcement settle FAILED.
evidence: Read monitor_flow.py census leg (announce census has no scheduler input; pending=missing, running=0 hard-coded at :222-228), dispatch.py _write_announcement (opposite-state marker removed only when the new attempt reaches its own terminal bookkeeping), recover_flow.py + grep: nothing anywhere clears announce markers on resubmit. auto_resume_flow.py:195-199's own comment ('the just-resumed (pending) jobs cannot trigger a same-generation re-entry') assumes walk-leg semantics that Phase 2 broke. Tests: test_flow_auto_resume.py/test_flow_resolve_and_recover.py drive only the walk leg (record_status patched, post-resume tick returns COMPLETE); test_flow_announce.py never combines announce census with a resubmit. Untested hazard; not in the refuted list or upstream-fixes plan (crash-only-monitoring.md drift log doesn't mention resubmit).
triage: Confirmed census leg (monitor_flow.py:219-239) has no scheduler input and hardcodes running=0/pending=missing; stale .failed markers after resubmit settle a live re-run FAILED. Not banked/refuted/fixed.

related.note: Same announce-census-no-scheduler-input seam, opposite triggers: F17/F24 = no marker so run never settles; F23 = stale .failed markers after a resubmit so a LIVE re-run is falsely settled FAILED and thrashes; F17's fix (announce/clear markers on resubmit) overlaps F23's remedy.

votes.reasoning: Verified the census leg hardcodes running=0/pending=missing and settles FAILED when complete+failed==total (classify.py:194-200); resubmit_failed extends job_ids but never bumps total_tasks (recover/runner.py:152-166), and nothing clears the .hpc/announce `.failed` markers on any resubmit path (only .hpc_failed/ preamble markers are cleared). So after a resubmit the stale `.failed` markers plus unchanged total keep the census at missing=0, re-settling a LIVE attempt-2 as FAILED. Executor-failure clusters (auto-recover + manual re-watch variants) reliably write `.failed` markers (dispatch.py:1332), making the LIVE-run-marked-FAILED / duplicate-array outcome solidly reachable; the auto-resume/preempted sub-variant is slightly narrower (hard-kills may skip the marker) but the core high-severity bug holds.

The census leg hardcodes running=0/pending=missing (monitor_flow.py:222-227) and classify_polling returns FAILED on running==0/pending==0/failed>0 (:194-200); recover_flow clears only $RESULT_DIR/.hpc_failed markers (:211-238) while dispatch flips the announce failed->complete marker only when the NEW attempt terminates, and grep confirms no resubmit path touches .hpc/announce/*. reconcile's fast path settles terminal from a full stale announcement before the alive-probe (:781-792). A resubmitted, live re-run is thus read FAILED on the next tick.

title: The submit-path preflight gate is inert: `hpc-agent preflight` exits 0 / ok:true even when every check fails, so submit-preflight reports overall='pass' and S1 briefs show a green preflight while the environment is broken
failure_scenario: Mid-campaign the user's ssh-agent key expires (or they typo the cluster name, or clusters.yaml still has <your_user> placeholders). /submit-hpc runs submit-s1 -> submit_preflight -> subprocess `hpc-agent preflight --cluster X`. check_preflight() correctly computes all_ok=False with failing checks (ssh_auth_sock, cluster_known, cluster_config_customized, the #275 runtime_uv gate), but the generic dispatcher wraps ANY return in _ok() and returns EXIT_OK, so the subresult is ok=true, overall='pass', and the S1 brief the human greenlights says preflight passed. Every guard the preflight family exists for (placeholder catch, wrong-cluster-name catch, dead-agent catch, uv-on-uvless-cluster catch) cannot fire on the primary block-drive path; the failure resurfaces as a raw rsync/auth error deep in S2 staging or dooms the array. check.py's own docstring ('The CLI adapter maps all_ok=False to the cluster-error exit code') describes an adapter that no longer exists.
fix_sketch: Give check-preflight a CliShape handler (or result_post + exit mapping) that emits the data but returns EXIT_CLUSTER_ERROR when all_ok is False — restoring the documented contract — OR make submit_preflight._run_subprocess fold `envelope.data.all_ok is False` into SubResult.ok for the check-preflight arm. Also stop truncating at submit_blocks.py:469: when overall != 'pass', include the failing checks' name+detail in brief['preflight'] so the human sees WHY, not just 'fail'.

Empirically ran in the repo venv: `hpc-agent preflight --cluster definitely-not-a-cluster` in a bare env -> EXIT 0, ok:true, all_ok:false with 4 failing checks; then real submit_preflight(cluster='definitely-not-a-cluster') -> overall:'pass' while check_preflight data.all_ok:false. Tests only pin the fiction: tests/ops/test_submit_preflight.py:190-205 mocks check-preflight failure as an ok:false ERROR envelope the real subprocess never emits; no test covers the all_ok:false/ok:true shape. Not in upstream-fixes-2026-07.md, bug-sweep refuted list, or the architecture review.
triage: Confirmed _dispatch.py:424-425 returns EXIT_OK unconditionally after result_post; check-preflight has no exit mapping, so all_ok:false yields ok:true/exit 0. Finder reports in-venv repro. Not banked.

related.note: Same failure class (a preflight-bearing CLI returns exit 0/ok:true despite all_ok=False) via different handlers: F29 = generic dispatch_primitive _ok() wrapper for the `preflight` verb; F31 = setup.py's own _setup_handler for `setup` (plus an independent dead-preflight-marker defect). Fixing one does not fix the other.

Ran the real composite: submit_preflight(cluster='definitely-not-a-cluster') returns overall='pass' while check_preflight.envelope.data.all_ok is False, because ok is derived as bool(envelope.get('ok')) (submit_preflight.py:297) and overall fails only when a subcall's ok is False (line 476). submit_blocks.py:469 folds only {'overall': ...} into the S1 brief, so the human greenlights a green preflight over a broken env. title: Renaming/moving the experiment directory mid-campaign silently forks the journal namespace: all run records, the decision journal, and the utterance log become invisible with no detection or remediation, wedging the campaign
failure_scenario: Day 3 of an array-batch campaign, the user renames ~/exp1 to ~/exp1-v2 (or reaches it via a different NFS automount path that resolve() doesn't canonicalize to the same string). repo_hash = sha256(resolved path)[:12], so every journal read (find_in_flight_runs, status-snapshot, block-drive resume cursor, read-decisions, the human-authorship utterance log, preflight markers) now targets a fresh empty ~/.claude/hpc/<newhash>/ — journal_dir() even scaffolds it with a new repo.json. Status reports nothing in flight while the array runs; block-drive has no parked marker; append-decision's authorship gate refuses against an empty utterance log with a remedy ('the human states the values') that never names the real cause; the detached watcher and the doctor cron (`hpc-agent-doctor-<old_hash>`, cwd = old path) keep writing to / failing against the old namespace. The old namespace's repo.json records the old experiment_dir, but no code path ever compares or relinks it — the fleet scan explicitly skips namespaces whose experiment_dir no longer exists 'silently' (attention_queue.py:1504). Recovery requires knowing the hash scheme and hand-moving dirs under ~/.claude/hpc.
fix_sketch: Detect the fork at journal_dir(): before scaffolding a fresh namespace, check whether <experiment_dir>/.hpc/runs/*.json sidecars reference run_ids absent from the new namespace and whether another namespace's repo.json claims a now-missing dir with matching run_ids — then either warn loudly with a relink command, or add a `hpc-agent relink-journal` verb. Longer-term: key the namespace on a durable token stored in .hpc/ (minted at first journal write) instead of the path hash, falling back to the path hash for old namespaces.
evidence: Read repo_hash/journal_dir (path-hash keyed, mkdir+repo.json on every call), index.py per-namespace lookups, utterances.py journal-side storage, and the attention-queue fleet scan's silent skip. Grepped all readers of repo.json: only the fleet scans read it, none compares it to a live dir or offers relink. The Windows path-form canonicalization (#296, run_record.py:352-394) shows the same-dir-different-string hazard is real and was only fixed for MINGW/WSL drive forms, not for renames or non-Windows aliasing. No test simulates a mid-campaign rename. Not covered by the three banked docs (checked for repo_hash/moved/rename).
triage: repo_hash is path-hash keyed; rename/aliasing forks the journal namespace with no detection. Windows-form fix (#296) is narrower. Not in banked docs.

related.note: Both stem from journal_dir()/repo_hash design (run_record.py:408-424): F30 = namespace keyed on the mutable resolved path, so a rename forks the journal; F46 = journal_dir() mkdirs+writes repo.json on read, so the existence guards can't fire and reads scaffold ghost namespaces. Different mechanisms/fixes, same accessor.

votes.reasoning: repo_hash = sha256(_canonicalize_for_hash(dir))[:12] and on non-Windows that is just str(Path(dir).resolve()) (run_record.py:373,405), so a rename or a genuinely different mount/automount path string yields a different hash. journal_dir (408-424) mkdirs and scaffolds a fresh repo.json on first access with no comparison to any existing namespace, and discover_fleet_experiments silently skips any repo.json whose experiment_dir no longer exists (attention_queue.py:1527-1528). I confirmed no code reads repo.json to relink or warn — only the fleet scans read it. The #296 Windows-form fix confirms the same-dir-different-string hazard was recognized but solved only for MINGW/WSL drive forms. Mechanism airtight; automount aliasing is a non-deliberate trigger, so 'high' holds despite being operator/environment-triggered.

title: scheduler_query_ran returns ran_ok=True for ANY rc on slurm/pbspro/torque — a down slurmctld or missing squeue/qstat binary (rc 1/126/127, stderr discarded) reads as 'queue empty', settling a healthy campaign as abandoned and false-confirming kills
failure_scenario: Mid multi-day SLURM campaign, slurmctld restarts for maintenance (or the login node exposes squeue only via login-shell modules — monitor commands run via `timeout ... bash -c` (remote.py:241-244), non-login, unlike submit's `bash -lc` in _remote_base.py:255-259). The alive query `squeue -j <ids> -h -o '%i' 2>/dev/null; echo __HPC_SCHED_ACK__=$?` yields empty stdout + ack rc=1/127. dialect explicit_id_liveness_query=True makes scheduler_query_ran return ("", True) regardless of rc, so parse_alive_output returns {} -> reconcile treats every job as having left the queue and classify.settle marks the run abandoned/terminal while all arrays are still running; kill's verify path (kill.py:127-137) likewise confirms 'N gone' after a scancel that never reached the controller. The engine's own header comment (_engine.py:50-56) claims the ack sentinel kills exactly this 'slurmctld down reads as no jobs' class — for the explicit-id families that guard can never fire.

For the daemon-down rc=1-with-empty-stdout case, stop discarding stderr blindly: have wrap_with_ack capture `head -c200` of stderr into a second sentinel line (or drop `2>/dev/null` and strip known 'Invalid job id'/'Unknown Job Id' lines in the parser) so 'slurm_load_jobs error: Unable to contact slurm controller' flips ran_ok False. Update tests/infra/test_scheduler_states.py which currently pins ack=35 + empty stdout -> ran_ok True for PBS (lines 83-98).
evidence: Read _engine.py:778-823 (rc ignored when explicit_id_liveness_query), profile.py:93-140 (dialect flags), reconcile.py:122-165 (empty parse = jobs gone; comment at :153 claims 'scheduler binary itself failed' is guarded — it isn't for these families), kill.py:127-147 (alive-set complement journaled as confirmed gone, then reconcile settles terminal), remote.py:212-244 (non-login bash -c wrapper; compound rc is the echo's 0). tests/infra/test_scheduler_states.py:83-98 pins the hazardous behavior (nonzero-rc empty output -> ran). Prior sweep bug #5's fix sketch itself flagged 'additionally check for rc==127' — not implemented; this is the overshoot residue of that fix, not a re-report.
triage: Confirmed _engine.py:821-822 returns ran_ok=True for explicit-id families regardless of rc, with no rc==127 guard; slurmctld-down/missing-binary reads as empty queue. Distinct overshoot of the landed #5 fix, not refuted.
severity_note: Note the core rc-semantics was an intentional #5 decision (docstring); finder's added claim is that rc 126/127 is distinguishable — a real gap for verifiers to weigh.

title: PBS Pro/TORQUE array job ids lose their '[]' at capture (JOB_ID_REGEX group 1 = digits only), so every later qstat -t / qdel / qstat -x -f -t addresses a job id that does not exist — arrays read as gone, kills false-confirm, history queries return nothing
