title: Retry after a crash or mid-plan wave failure re-qsubs already-landed arrays: the crash-safety pre-stamp is written but no submit path ever reads it, so the same run_id ends up running twice and the first arrays become untracked ghosts
failure_scenario: Over-cap sweep splits into 6 waves; wave 4's sbatch dies on an SSH flake (the flaky-network case #339 inc 4 anticipates). _submit_one_spec pre-stamps the 3 landed wave job_ids onto the sidecar and re-raises; the journal record never lands (submit_and_record is post-qsub). The user does the natural thing and re-runs the same submit-flow spec: _dedup_existing (2215) consults ONLY the journal → miss; _ensure_run_sidecar accepts the existing sidecar; the retry re-submits ALL 6 waves. Waves 0-2 now run TWICE under the same run_id — two arrays concurrently writing the same result_dir_template paths (nondeterministic clobber of results) — and update_run_sidecar_job_ids (state/runs.py:1007, 'sets job_ids') then replaces the pre-stamped ids with the new 6, so the original 3 arrays are untracked by any monitor/kill/reconcile path forever. The same fires for the ≤cap single-array kill in the qsub→record window (the 2026-06-11 demo's exact window, per the comment at 2558-2564). The escape hatches cannot fire: layer-2 cmd_sha dedup is default-off in submit-flow (dedup_cmd_sha=None unless invalidate_on_code_change, 2590-2592), it runs AFTER the duplicate qsub anyway, and runner.py:508-517 deliberately excludes the run's OWN sidecar from the A5 match — so is_orphan_sidecar's stated contract ('sidecar with job_ids and no journal → submit_and_record will reconstruct', runs.py:933-936) is unreachable on this path. Repo-rule violation: a documented recovery guard that cannot fire.
fix_sketch: In _submit_flow_batch_locked (or _dedup_existing), before treating a spec as fresh, read the spec's sidecar: if it carries non-empty job_ids but no journal record, do NOT qsub — either reconstruct the journal record from the sidecar (the A5 reconstruction in runner.py already knows how, ~line 559) and return deduped=True, or refuse with a typed error naming the landed job_ids and pointing at reconcile/kill. Tests currently pin only that the exception CARRIES partial accounting (tests/ops/submit/test_multiwave_submit.py:273, tests/infra/backends/test_submit_plan_partial_and_canary.py:153); no test drives a retry after the partial failure.
evidence: Read submit_flow.py 2215-2234 (journal-only dedup), 2536-2577 (both pre-stamps), 2590-2592 (cmd_sha threaded only under invalidate_on_code_change), runner.py 471-517 (A5 gated on cmd_sha; own-sidecar match explicitly skipped), state/runs.py 1007-1037 (stamp replaces job_ids) and 921-949 (is_orphan_sidecar's reconstruct contract). Grepped docs/plans/upstream-fixes-2026-07.md, bug-sweep-2026-07-11.md, architecture-review-2026-07-13.md for partial_submit/double-submit/pre-stamp: not covered. No test exercises re-running submit-flow after a partial failure.
triage: Verified _dedup_existing submit_flow.py:2217-2234 consults only load_run (journal), never the sidecar's landed job_ids. Re-qsub-after-partial defect present.

votes.reasoning: Read _dedup_existing (journal-only, 2217), the multiwave partial-failure pre-stamp+re-raise (2059-2074, 2543-2557), _ensure_run_sidecar (no-op on a valid pre-stamped sidecar, never blocks on job_ids), and the A5 fallback (cmd_sha None by default, runs post-qsub, own-sidecar excluded 508-517). A retry after a mid-plan wave failure re-qsubs all waves under the same run_id and update_run_sidecar_job_ids replaces the pre-stamped ids, orphaning the first arrays. Every link holds; over-cap sweep + SSH flake + natural retry is a routine multi-day-campaign scenario.

Confirmed _dedup_existing (submit_flow.py:2217) consults only load_run (journal); the multi-wave partial-failure path (2536-2557) pre-stamps landed job_ids to the sidecar and re-raises without ever writing the journal record, so a natural re-run of the same spec misses dedup and re-qsubs ALL waves — waves 0-2 run twice under one run_id, clobbering shared result dirs. The post-qsub update_run_sidecar_job_ids (runs.py:1007) rewrites/replaces job_ids, orphaning the original arrays, and the only reconstruction guard (runner.py:508-517 A5) is gated on cmd_sha (default-off in submit-flow) AND explicitly skips the run's own pre-stamped sidecar. A documented recovery guard that structurally cannot fire on this path.

title: submit-flow's front-door dedup ignores cluster, so a retarget of an in-flight run_id to a different cluster silently 'succeeds' as deduped=True against the OLD cluster — the proving-run-5 cross-cluster REFUSE guard in runner._resolve_layer1 cannot fire on the primary workflow path
failure_scenario: Cluster A degrades mid-campaign; the user rebuilds the same spec targeting cluster B (same swept params → same run_id, since run_id keys on parameters only, #207) and calls submit-flow / submit-flow-batch. _dedup_existing loads the journal record (status in_flight on A) and, being not resubmittable-terminal, returns SubmitFlowResult(deduped=True, job_ids=A's job ids) with NO cluster comparison — before any gate runs (deduped specs skip supersession/sidecar/prelude entirely, submit_flow.py:2864-2870). The retarget silently did not happen: nothing runs on B, the envelope reports success-shaped dedup, and the campaign keeps monitoring the degraded A run. runner._resolve_layer1's in_flight_cluster_mismatch REFUSE (229-236, built for exactly this after proving run #5) sits downstream in submit_and_record, which the flow only reaches for FRESH specs after qsub — so on the main agent-facing surface the guard is dead code for this scenario.
fix_sketch: Mirror the layer-1 cluster check in _dedup_existing: when existing is non-terminal and both existing.cluster and spec.cluster are non-empty and differ, raise the same SpecInvalid the runner raises (runner.py:430-440) instead of returning a deduped result. Reuse _resolve_layer1 directly (it is pure) so the two seats cannot drift — the twin-predicate class the G11 generator in upstream-fixes names.
evidence: Read _dedup_existing (no cluster field consulted) and _resolve_layer1/_REFUSE plus its raise site (runner.py:229-236, 429-440). tests/ops/submit/test_layer1_dedup.py:122 pins only the pure function's decision; no test drives a cross-cluster in_flight retarget through submit_flow/_dedup_existing. Not listed in the sweep's Refuted section or the architecture review (grepped 'retarget', '_dedup_existing').
triage: Verified _dedup_existing has no cluster comparison; returns deduped for any non-resubmittable-terminal record. Cross-cluster retarget silently deduped against old cluster.

votes.reasoning: _dedup_existing makes no cluster comparison and returns deduped=True for any non-resubmittable-terminal record; deduped specs short-circuit before submit_and_record (2864-2870), where the in_flight_cluster_mismatch REFUSE (runner 234-235/429-440) lives. Since run_id keys on parameters only (#207, corroborated across the code and by the REFUSE guard's own reason), a cluster-B retarget of an in_flight-on-A run silently dedups against A. The proving-run-#5 guard is dead on the primary submit-flow surface.

_dedup_existing (submit_flow.py:2215-2234), invoked up front for every spec at line 2864, returns deduped=True with the old record's job_ids for any non-resubmittable-terminal record and never reads spec.cluster; this short-circuits before submit_and_record/_resolve_layer1 where the in_flight_cluster_mismatch REFUSE (runner.py:234-235) lives. The runner comment itself states run_id is parameter-only and cross-cluster retarget is exactly the scenario that guard was built for, so the guard is dead on the primary submit-flow path — a retarget silently no-ops as success-shaped dedup.

title: Deploy-cache manifest rides the SAME non-atomic transfer as the files it attests and lands FIRST on the wire, so an interrupted deploy_runtime + retry reports success while the cluster runs stale or torn framework code
failure_scenario: User upgrades hpc-agent mid-campaign (pkg_version changes -> full redeploy of dispatcher/combiner/templates). deploy_runtime batches the NEW manifest (.hpc/.deploy_state.json) into the same transfer as the files (transport.py:1988-2000). On the rsync leg, rsync sorts the file list — '.deploy_state.json' (0x2E) sorts before '_hpc_combiner.py'/'_hpc_dispatch.py'/'templates' — so the manifest is literally the FIRST file transferred, and '--inplace' (transport.py:1806) forgoes per-file atomicity. A network flap / the 60s SSH_TIMEOUT (transport.py:1807) / Ctrl-C after the manifest lands but before the code does raises loud — but the user's natural retry then reads the already-landed NEW manifest in the prelude (transport.py:1956-1977), every sha 'matches', to_deploy is empty, manifest_changed is False, and deploy_runtime returns SUCCESS having shipped nothing. The cluster silently keeps the PREVIOUS version's dispatcher/combiner/templates (or --inplace-torn files) while the control plane believes the new version is deployed — exactly the version-skew the pkg_version cache key exists to prevent. A torn dispatch.py at least dies loudly at canary; a stale-but-valid old combiner/reporter silently produces old-behavior aggregation for the rest of the campaign. The tar fallback extracts in readdir order — same hazard.
fix_sketch: Never let the manifest ride the file transfer: drop the manifest from transfer_items and write it in a separate small ssh leg AFTER _deploy_transfer returns success (the exact pattern _write_push_manifest at transport.py:763-783 already implements — base64-piped, fail-open). Alternatively rsync with '--delay-updates' instead of '--inplace' plus a manifest-last second pass. Add a test: simulate a transfer that raises after the manifest item is 'delivered', retry deploy_runtime, assert the changed files are re-shipped.
evidence: Read deploy_runtime end-to-end at d731b12. The in-code safety claim (transport.py:1983-1986: 'a failed transfer raises before anything is recorded, so a stale manifest never claims a file landed that didn't') only holds for the local list-append order, not wire order — rsync sends its sorted file list and '.deploy_state.json' sorts first in .hpc/. tests/infra/test_deploy_runtime_cache.py covers cache hit/miss/corrupt-manifest but has NO interrupted-transfer case (grep for partial/interrupt/fail: none). Not in the 2026-07-11 sweep (#66 was the manifest being wiped by push --delete — the opposite direction) nor the architecture review.
triage: Verified transport.py:1988-1998 appends manifest last to local list; comment (1983-1986) only reasons about local order, not rsync's sorted wire order where '.deploy_state.json' sorts first under --inplace. Interrupted-deploy stale-manifest gap present.

related.note: Shared non-atomic deploy_runtime --inplace transfer (transport.py:1786-1820), distinct mechanisms: F20 = a concurrent running array reads a torn dispatcher mid-transfer; F53 = the cache manifest ships in the same transfer and lands first, so an interrupted deploy+retry no-ops and runs stale/torn code. Both fixed by making the deploy atomic; not a single defect.

votes.reasoning: Read deploy_runtime end-to-end. _DEPLOY_MANIFEST_REL='.hpc/.deploy_state.json' (1606) sorts first in rsync's sorted/incremental transfer (.hpc before hpc_agent; .deploy_state.json before _hpc_*/templates), with hpc_agent/ reporter+combiner files last; --inplace (1806) is non-atomic. On retry the prelude cat reads the complete landed manifest, all shas match, transfer_items empties, deploy returns success shipping nothing while stale hpc_agent code persists. The in-code safety comment (1983-1986) reasons only about local list order, not wire order. Ctrl-C (KeyboardInterrupt) bypasses the backoff since it only retries TimeoutError, giving a clean trigger; the dangerous window spans the entire code transfer after the tiny manifest lands.

Verified _DEPLOY_MANIFEST_REL='.hpc/.deploy_state.json' and that it byte-sorts before every shipped code file (. < _ < letters; .hpc before hpc_agent), so under rsync's sorted flist with --inplace (transport.py:1806) the manifest lands durably first; an interruption after it lands makes the retry read the new manifest, compute to_deploy=[] and manifest_changed=False, ship nothing and return success while stale/torn code runs. The tar fallback is a direct-overwrite tar x of the same bundled stream (:1143,1171) — same hazard — and the safety comment (:1983-1986) reasons only about local list order, not wire order. Couldn't run rsync in-sandbox but its flist sorting is standard and the ordering is arithmetically confirmed.

title: _with_ssh_backoff retries client-side TimeoutError for non-idempotent submit commands while build_remote_command grants the first attempt's remote half timeout+60s to complete — a slow qmaster turns one submit_one into up to 3 duplicate qsub/sbatch array submissions, only the last of which is tracked
failure_scenario: Overloaded SGE/UGE qmaster or NFS-lagged login node makes qsub take 60-120s (a routine event on Hoffman2-class clusters). submit_one rides the default ssh_run (remote_factory.py:69: no timeout arg -> SSH_TIMEOUT_SEC=60). Attempt 1 times out client-side at 60s and raises TimeoutError — but the remote half runs under 'timeout -k 10 <60+60>s bash -c qsub ...' (REMOTE_DEADLINE_MARGIN_SEC=60, remote.py:134/188-194), so the first qsub completes at, say, 75s and SUBMITS THE ARRAY; its job id is discarded. _ssh_backoff_policy retries TimeoutError (retry_on=(TimeoutError, _ThrottleRetry), remote.py:322): attempt 2 re-runs qsub 2s later. Best case: attempt 2 succeeds fast, the run records attempt 2's job id, and attempt 1's array is an untracked duplicate running the identical tasks and double-writing the same results/<run_id> dirs — silently corrupted metrics and doubled allocation burn. Worst case: 3 consecutive timeouts open the breaker (guarded_call records TimeoutError as a connection failure, ssh_circuit.py:453-455), the verb fails loud, and up to 3 phantom arrays are already queued; the user re-runs submit and adds a 4th. verify_submitted only checks recorded job ids, so the strays are invisible.
fix_sketch: Make timeout-retry conditional on idempotence: thread a retry_on_timeout=False (or op-class) flag through ssh_run into _with_ssh_backoff and set it for _run_in_repo/submit_one (the qsub/sbatch leg) so a client timeout on submit surfaces immediately instead of re-executing; and/or before any submit retry, query the scheduler for a job matching the just-submitted job_name (the backends already have job_name-scoped queries) and adopt its id instead of resubmitting. Keep timeout-retry for the idempotent probe/status/pull surfaces.
evidence: Read _with_ssh_backoff + _ssh_backoff_policy (remote.py:308-420: TimeoutError is an explicitly retryable trigger), build_remote_command/_remote_deadline_seconds (client budget + 60s margin keeps the first remote qsub alive past the client's give-up), and _run_in_repo (_remote_base.py:248/260) which submits via the injected default ssh_run with no timeout/no-retry override and no duplicate detection (grep for TimeoutError/idempot/duplicate in backends: none). Sweep #16 covered duplicate resubmits at the recover_flow resume-marker layer only; the sweep's refuted list has nothing on this seam; no test exercises a timed-out-then-retried submit.
triage: Verified _ssh_backoff_policy remote.py:322 retry_on=(TimeoutError, _ThrottleRetry); with the +60s remote deadline margin a slow qsub re-executes. Duplicate-submit defect present.

related.note: Both cause duplicate remote execution of a non-idempotent submit at different SSH layers and compound: F54 = _with_ssh_backoff retries client TimeoutError while the remote qsub still lands; F55 = engine EngineUnavailable -> one-shot re-exec of an already-dispatched command; F55 notes the backoff can then retry the one-shot on top.

votes.reasoning: remote_factory.py:69 binds submit's ssh_run with no timeout -> 60s client, and build_remote_command wraps qsub in 'timeout -k 10 120s' (client 60 + margin 60). The framework documents (remote.py:109-121) that a client-killed ssh orphans a still-running remote half, so a 75s qsub submits its array after the 60s client TimeoutError; _ssh_backoff_policy retry_on=(TimeoutError,...) (322) then resubmits, and _run_in_repo has no duplicate/idempotence guard. This is the default (non-engine) path, so no opt-in is required; a slow qmaster yields an untracked duplicate array.

Re-derived the deadline arithmetic: _remote_deadline_seconds returns int(timeout)+REMOTE_DEADLINE_MARGIN_SEC(=60), and build_remote_command wraps the remote qsub in `timeout -k 10 <client+60>s bash -c ...`, deliberately outliving the client (the orphan-bound design premise at remote.py:120-134). submit_one rides remote_factory.py:69's ssh_run with no timeout override -> SSH_TIMEOUT_SEC=60s client budget, and _ssh_backoff_policy (remote.py:322) lists TimeoutError as retryable. A qsub completing in the [60s,120s] window queues the array while the client already gave up and discards the job id, then the ladder resubmits — a duplicate untracked array double-writing results/<run_id>. No idempotence/duplicate guard on the submit leg (grep confirms none); verify_submitted only checks recorded ids. The race window is probabilistic but the code comments themselves document multi-second-to-22s login-node variance, so a 60s+ qsub is within reach on an overloaded qmaster.

title: Engine seam silently RE-EXECUTES a command that was already dispatched to the remote: any mid-command engine failure (per-command asyncssh timeout, torn connection) raises EngineUnavailable, and ssh_run's 'except EngineUnavailable: pass' re-runs the same command one-shot — the un-landed half of the #8 fix
failure_scenario: HPC_SSH_ENGINE=asyncssh opted in (the recent l-engine work this seam exists for). A submit/combiner/journal-touching command is dispatched on the persistent connection; then either (a) the per-command asyncssh timeout fires (slow login node — the command is genuinely still RUNNING remotely, and the server-side self-destruct gives it another 60s), or (b) a NAT/Wi-Fi flap tears the connection just as the remote half finishes (result in flight). _Engine.run's except-Exception branch (ssh_engine.py:583-595) discards the connection and raises EngineUnavailable; remote.py:735-736 catches it with a bare 'pass' and falls through to the one-shot path, RE-EXECUTING the full remote command — then _with_ssh_backoff can retry the one-shot timeouts on top (finding above), compounding to several executions. For qsub that is a duplicate untracked array; for a combiner --force leg it is a concurrent double-writer to _combiner/wave_N.json mid-campaign. The caller's wall-clock also silently doubles (engine timeout + fresh one-shot timeout).
fix_sketch: Distinguish 'engine could not serve the call' (breaker-refused/failed connect, wedged loop BEFORE dispatch — safe to fall back) from 'command was already dispatched' (per-command timeout, ConnectionLost during run). E.g. a dispatched=True attribute or an EngineCommandFailed subclass raised from _Engine.run's post-dispatch except; in remote.py's seam, re-raise dispatched failures as TimeoutError/RemoteCommandFailed instead of falling through, or gate the fallback re-execution on an idempotence flag threaded from the caller (same flag as the backoff fix).
evidence: Read _Engine.run (all post-dispatch exceptions collapse to the single EngineUnavailable type, ssh_engine.py:583-595) and the seam (remote.py:731-736). Sweep #8's landed fix is only the inflight sweeper veto (ssh_engine.py:507, 692-714 + test_sweep_idle_does_not_sever_an_inflight_command); its fix-sketch explicitly left 'distinguish already-dispatched failures so mid-command engine deaths do not silently re-execute' as an OPTIONAL extra, and grep shows no such distinction landed. tests/infra/test_remote.py:996 pins 'EngineUnavailable → fall through to one-shot' generically — the dispatched case is pinned WRONG, not merely untested.
triage: Verified remote.py:735-736 `except EngineUnavailable: pass` falls through to one-shot, and ssh_engine.py:583-595 collapses all post-dispatch exceptions to EngineUnavailable. Undifferentiated re-execution present.

related.note: Both cause duplicate remote execution of a non-idempotent submit at different SSH layers and compound: F54 = _with_ssh_backoff retries client TimeoutError while the remote qsub still lands; F55 = engine EngineUnavailable -> one-shot re-exec of an already-dispatched command; F55 notes the backoff can then retry the one-shot on top.

Compounding within the ssh engine: F56 (_discard closes the shared connection under in-flight peers) severs sibling commands mid-flight, which then each hit F55's post-dispatch EngineUnavailable -> silent one-shot re-execution. Distinct root causes (unconditional discard vs no already-dispatched distinction).

votes.reasoning: ssh_engine.py:583-595 collapses every post-dispatch failure (asyncssh per-command timeout with partial stdout, torn connection) into one EngineUnavailable, and remote.py:735-736 'except EngineUnavailable: pass' falls through to the one-shot _with_ssh_backoff path (784), re-executing a command already dispatched to the remote. No dispatched-vs-undispatched distinction exists. Gated on HPC_SSH_ENGINE=asyncssh opt-in, which the finding states; the mechanism is exact and high-impact for qsub/combiner.

ssh_engine._Engine.run dispatches at line 578 (_submit(_do_run(hc,cmd,...))) then its `except Exception` at 583-595 collapses per-command asyncssh timeouts AND torn-connection failures — genuinely post-dispatch, command still running remotely under the timeout wrapper — into the same EngineUnavailable as pre-dispatch channel-open refusals, with no dispatched/undispatched distinction. ssh_run (remote.py:735-736) catches EngineUnavailable with bare `pass` and falls through to the one-shot path, re-executing the full remote_cmd -> duplicate qsub / concurrent combiner double-writer, then F54's ladder can retry on top. This is exactly the 'distinguish already-dispatched failures' extra that #8's landed inflight-sweeper veto left un-implemented. Gated behind the opt-in HPC_SSH_ENGINE=asyncssh (not the default path), which the finding scopes honestly; impact-when-fired justifies high.

title: Mid-call liveness heartbeat is silently swallowed under the default in-process runner: redirect_stderr captures the daemon thread's '[mcp] still running' lines, resurrecting the exact 'is it hung?' blindness it was shipped to fix
failure_scenario: Any long MCP tool call (a detached-launch submit-s2, a doctor scan, an aggregate-check over slow NFS) under the DEFAULT _in_process_cli_runner: the heartbeat thread (:1523-1529, added for run-#12 finding 3 — 'three separate is-it-hung investigations in one night') resolves `sys.stderr` at write time, but _in_process_cli_runner holds contextlib.redirect_stderr(err) (:939) which rebinds sys.stderr process-wide across ALL threads for the call's duration. Every 15s heartbeat line lands in the captured StringIO instead of the tail-able MCP log; _tool_result then discards captured stderr unless the envelope is unparseable (:1232-1240). Net: during exactly the long calls the feature exists for, the human tailing the MCP stderr log sees nothing between dispatch and result — and combined with the unguarded blocking verbs above, a wedged server is also a silent one. The lines DO appear under the subprocess runner, so the behavior diverges between the parity-checked runners.
fix_sketch: Capture the real stderr stream object once at heartbeat-thread start (e.g. `real_err = sys.stderr` in _invoke_cli before invoking the runner, and have _heartbeat write to that saved handle), or write via `sys.__stderr__`. Alternatively make _in_process_cli_runner capture stderr with a thread-local-safe writer instead of process-global redirect. Add a test that runs a slow fake verb in-process and asserts the heartbeat line reaches the pre-redirect stderr object.
evidence: Read _invoke_cli's heartbeat thread and _in_process_cli_runner's redirect_stderr at d731b12; redirect_stderr swaps the sys module attribute globally (all threads). Default runner confirmed at McpServer.__init__ (:1308) and cmd_mcp_serve (src/hpc_agent/cli/mcp.py:104-107, no runner override). Grepped tests/test_mcp_server.py, test_mcp_curated.py, _mcp_harness.py for 'heartbeat'/'still running': zero hits — untested hazard. Not in the 2026-07-11 sweep or the architecture review.
triage: Heartbeat-under-redirect_stderr observability gap; not in any doc; real operator failure mode (silent long calls). Not refuted/banked/fixed.

votes.reasoning: The heartbeat thread resolves sys.stderr at write time (line 1525) but the default _in_process_cli_runner wraps the runner in contextlib.redirect_stderr(err), which rebinds the process-global sys.stderr across all threads for the call's duration; _tool_result (1229-1246) only surfaces stderr when the envelope is unparseable. So heartbeat lines are captured into the discarded StringIO during exactly the long in-process calls the feature exists for, while the subprocess runner (no server-side redirect) shows them — a real observability divergence.

title: CONTRACT.md retry-policy table misclassifies outputs_missing as user/retry_safe-false; the wire actually emits category=cluster, retry_safe=true, exit 2 — an orchestrator built from the doc mis-dispatches on a routine mid-campaign error
failure_scenario: A multi-day array campaign where some tasks died before writing outputs: `aggregate --require-outputs` fails with outputs_missing — a common, expected mid-campaign event. CONTRACT.md's table (the doc that tells external harnesses 'read this file ... and you have the full surface', and whose exit-code section says 'Dispatch on exit code BEFORE parsing JSON') declares outputs_missing category=user / retry_safe=false → the exit-1 caller-fixable class per the same doc's mapping. The actual envelope carries category='cluster', retry_safe=True → exit 2. An orchestrator coded from the doc routes the exit-2 result into its cluster backoff-retry branch (or trips an assertion on the unexpected exit code) and loops re-running aggregate against outputs that will never appear, instead of executing the real remediation (resubmit the listed task ids) — hours of wasted polling/cluster time mid-campaign. The reverse harness (trusting the envelope) contradicts the doc it was audited against.
fix_sketch: Fix the CONTRACT.md row to cluster / true with the actual recommended action ('resubmit the listed task_ids, then re-run aggregate' — matching OutputsMissing.remediation in errors.py:298-302). Better: generate the table from the HpcError hierarchy (the doc already names errors.py as source of truth at CONTRACT.md:99) so rows cannot drift — the same generate-don't-hand-maintain posture the architecture review prescribes for cli-spec.md's tables.
evidence: Read CONTRACT.md:117 ('outputs_missing | user | false') vs errors.py OutputsMissing (retry_safe=True, category='cluster') and cli-spec.md:113 (outputs_missing in the exit-2 cluster row — the two docs also contradict each other) at d731b12. No doc-sync test: tests/integration/test_external_harness_compat.py asserts live envelope semantics only, never the CONTRACT.md table. Dedup checked: architecture-review P6 item 9 enumerates the kill/cancel contradiction, the 17-vs-18 code count, and the timeout-enum conflation — this row is not among them; not in the 2026-07-11 sweep or upstream-fixes.
triage: Verified CONTRACT.md:117 (user/false) vs errors.py:295-297 OutputsMissing (cluster/retry_safe=True). Architecture-review E2 lists kill/17-vs-18/timeout CONTRACT contradictions but NOT this row; external-orchestrator mis-dispatch is a runtime consequence.

votes.reasoning: errors.py:287-302 OutputsMissing is retry_safe=True/category='cluster' (exit 2), and cli-spec.md:113 correctly places outputs_missing in the exit-2 cluster row, but CONTRACT.md:117 declares it user/false (the exit-1 caller-fixable class). The doc contradicts both the code (its own named source of truth) and the sibling spec; an external orchestrator coded from CONTRACT.md mis-routes a routine mid-campaign error away from the resubmit-and-retry remediation.

title: MCP prompts/get serves the Claude-Code slash bodies verbatim — instructing an MCP-only client to use the Skill tool, Bash, and CronCreate it structurally lacks — while the MCP-executable start_instruction is only the never-taken fallback
failure_scenario: An MCP-first harness (the server's own safety premise: 'An MCP client has no shell — it can only call the verbs this server exposes', mcp_server.py:30-33) pulls prompts/get 'submit-hpc' to start a campaign. get_prompt returns the packaged slash .md body whenever installed (it is package data, so always); that body's operative instructions are 'Invoke the hpc-submit skill via the Skill tool ... the skill body owns the block invocation (do not shell it from this slash)'. monitor-hpc.md additionally instructs running `hpc-agent monitor-summary` in bash and passing cron_create_args to a CronCreate tool. None of these exist for an MCP client: no Skill tool, no shell, no CronCreate. The agent either dead-ends or improvises — hand-authoring specs against raw curated verbs, the finding-13/17 spec-corruption class the curated catalog's own comments (:156-267) document as the thing that corrupts runs. The table-projected start_instruction (workflow_entries.py:63-74) — 'invoke block-drive ... commit via append-decision', fully executable over the curated MCP surface — is only returned when the .md is MISSING (mcp_server.py:1721-1731), i.e. never in a normal install.
fix_sketch: Invert get_prompt's preference: return entry.start_instruction as the message body always (it is the §6 canonical entry), keeping the .md only for the human `description` field — the docstring at :1700-1704 already says the table is the source of truth either way. This also de-risks the workflow_entries.py:23-28 TODO(wave4) without waiting for the slash-body deletion pass.
evidence: Read get_prompt and _read_command_md at d731b12; read all five commands/*.md — every workflow body routes through Skill()/bash/CronCreate. tests/test_mcp_server.py:286-292 pins only that prompts/get returns non-empty text, not that it is executable in-context. The architecture review's P6 covers doc ghosts and two missing slash files but not this MCP-prompt/slash-body projection inversion; the workflow_entries TODO acknowledges the second copy but not the runtime mis-direction consequence.
triage: MCP prompts/get serving slash bodies referencing Skill/Bash/CronCreate an MCP client lacks; workflow_entries TODO acknowledges the copy but not the runtime mis-direction. Real dead-end.

votes.reasoning: Reproduced: get_prompt('submit-hpc') returns the packaged slash .md body that instructs Skill("hpc-submit", ...); monitor-hpc.md further references bash hpc-agent monitor-summary and CronCreate/CronDelete. get_prompt only returns the MCP-executable start_instruction when _read_command_md returns None, i.e. when the .md is absent — but it ships as package data so it is present in a normal install. An MCP-only client lacking Skill/shell/CronCreate dead-ends or improvises against raw verbs.

title: Incremental _combiner pull never refreshes a wave that was re-combined with --force on the cluster: the stale local partial is reduced and persisted as the durable aggregate
failure_scenario: User discovers wave 3 combined over partial data, fixes the tasks, and runs the CLI's documented remediation `hpc-agent aggregate --run-id R --wave 3 --force` — the cluster's wave_3.json is rewritten with the full data. Next aggregate-flow call: _incremental_include_patterns computes needed = combined_waves − waves-present-locally-BY-FILENAME; wave 3's old copy exists locally, so the rsync include filter ('wave_NONE_SENTINEL.json' when everything is 'already pulled') excludes it from transfer entirely — rsync's own delta detection never gets a chance. reduce_partials runs over the stale local file, _persist_local_aggregate writes the stale numbers into _aggregated/<run_id>/metrics_aggregate.json (the verify-reproduction comparator), and collect_wave_errors re-raises the stale 'incomplete' escalation. The user believes they repaired the aggregate; the numbers did not change and nothing says why.
fix_sketch: Stop excluding locally-present waves from the transfer: always include wave_*.json / wave_*.runtime.json patterns and let rsync's checksum/mtime delta skip unchanged files (the narrowing was an argv-size optimization; the pattern list can stay small by including all waves with two patterns). Alternatively, record a per-wave combine timestamp in the journal when combine_wave runs with force=True and drop the local copy for those waves before the pull.
evidence: Read _incremental_include_patterns (returns the non-matching sentinel when needed is empty; diff is by filename only, no mtime/sha) and rsync_pull (include list + trailing --exclude=* means excluded files are never even compared). tests/ops/aggregate/test_flow_incremental_pull.py pins the skip-already-pulled behavior as intended but has no case where the remote partial changed after the local copy landed. combine-wave's --force help text (combine.py:63-66) is the documented remediation this defeats.
triage: Incremental pull filename-only diff excludes force-recombined waves; novel local/remote divergence. Not fixed/banked/refuted.

related.note: Shared defect: _incremental_include_patterns diffs waves by filename only, so a locally-present-but-wrong wave is never re-pulled — F08 via a force-recombined remote change, F09 via a torn/corrupt local file. F09 adds an independent reduce-swallows-JSONDecodeError silent-drop, so related rather than identical.

votes.reasoning: _incremental_include_patterns (aggregate_flow.py:203-224) diffs combined_waves against locally-present files by filename only, and rsync_pull appends a trailing --exclude=* after the include list (transport.py:2197-2202) so a locally-present wave is excluded from transfer entirely — rsync's own mtime/checksum delta never runs. A cluster wave re-combined via the documented `aggregate --wave N --force` remediation is thus never re-pulled and the stale local copy is reduced and persisted. Reachable but gated on the operator using the force remediation; medium is right.

title: A torn/corrupt local wave partial is silently dropped from the reduce AND permanently pinned by the incremental pull — an entire wave vanishes from the aggregate with no disclosure
failure_scenario: aggregate-flow's pull runs over the scp fallback (Windows control plane, transport.py:2182-2195 — scp writes in place, unlike rsync's tmp+rename) and is interrupted mid-file by a network drop or the bounded timeout, leaving a truncated _aggregated/<run>/_combiner/wave_7.json; a local disk-full write produces the same state. reduce_partials hits json.JSONDecodeError and `continue`s — wave 7's grid_points silently drop out of aggregated_metrics — and collect_wave_errors ALSO `continue`s on the unreadable file, so incomplete_waves stays empty and escalation_reason reports nothing. On every subsequent aggregate-flow call, _incremental_include_patterns counts wave_7.json as present by FILENAME, excludes it from the rsync include list, and the intact remote copy is never re-pulled: the wave is missing from the numbers for the rest of the campaign. (The cluster-side _final_reduce path handles this correctly — an unreadable wave lands in errors_per_wave/incomplete_waves, combiner.py:276-283 — the local default path does not.)
fix_sketch: Mirror the cluster-final behavior locally: make collect_wave_errors record an unreadable/unparseable wave_<N>.json as that wave's error (it already parses the wave number from the filename), so it lands in incomplete_waves and escalation_reason; and make _incremental_include_patterns treat a locally-unparseable wave file as not-present so the next pull repairs it (a cheap json.loads or size>0-and-endswith-'}' probe over the handful of local partials).
evidence: Read reduce_partials/collect_wave_errors (both swallow JSONDecodeError with continue; only the errors field INSIDE a parseable file is surfaced), _incremental_include_patterns (filename regex only), _scp_pull dispatch in rsync_pull, and _final_reduce's contrasting unreadable-wave handling. No test feeds a corrupt wave file to reduce_partials or to the incremental-pull diff (checked tests/ops/aggregate/test_flow_incremental_pull.py and tests for reduce metrics).
triage: Torn local wave partial swallowed by JSONDecodeError continue + pinned by filename-only incremental diff; cluster path handles it, local does not. Novel.

related.note: Shared defect: _incremental_include_patterns diffs waves by filename only, so a locally-present-but-wrong wave is never re-pulled — F08 via a force-recombined remote change, F09 via a torn/corrupt local file. F09 adds an independent reduce-swallows-JSONDecodeError silent-drop, so related rather than identical.

votes.reasoning: reduce_partials (metrics.py:246) and collect_wave_errors (273) both `continue` on json.JSONDecodeError/OSError with no record, so a torn/corrupt local wave silently drops from aggregated_metrics and never reaches incomplete_waves/escalation_reason — while the cluster _final_reduce (combiner.py:280-283) records it in errors_per_wave/incomplete_waves (confirmed divergence). The same wave is then pinned by the filename-only incremental diff. Trigger (scp fallback interruption or local disk-full producing a truncated file) is narrower than rsync's atomic tmp+rename path, so medium is appropriate.

title: Local _aggregated/ (pulled per-task mirrors, summaries, fingerprint/reproduction ledgers) is in no push exclude set: every re-submit rsyncs it back to the cluster and delete=True wipes the cluster-side _aggregated/ tree
failure_scenario: aggregate-flow lands its outputs at <experiment_dir>/_aggregated/<run_id>/ — including _per_task_results/ (a mirror of every per-task summary the fallback reduce pulled, potentially GBs on a large sweep) plus _fingerprints/, _conformance/ and reproduction ledgers. `_aggregated` appears in neither DEFAULT_RSYNC_EXCLUDES nor PROTECTED_OUTPUT_DIRS nor MANDATORY_RSYNC_EXCLUDES, so the next submit in the campaign pushes the whole tree to remote_path — multiplying deploy time (the exact 1800s-timeout class finding 19 fought on the pull side, now recreated on the push side, and on the rsync-less delta path every one of those files is manifest-hashed) — and, because the push runs with delete semantics against a local tree that does not mirror the cluster-side _aggregated/ written by the cluster final-reduce, the pre-clean/--delete replaces or removes the cluster's own aggregate artifacts. Mid-campaign symptom: submits get slower each iteration and evidence ledgers leak onto the shared cluster filesystem.
fix_sketch: Add "_aggregated/" to PROTECTED_OUTPUT_DIRS (it is a run-output dir on the cluster side, written by the final reduce, exactly like _combiner/) — that both stops the pre-clean/--delete from touching the remote copy and, via the push exclude union, stops the local pulled tree from being shipped up.
evidence: Read DEFAULT_RSYNC_EXCLUDES / PROTECTED_OUTPUT_DIRS / MANDATORY_RSYNC_EXCLUDES and _effective_excludes (transport.py:155+), submit_flow._push_and_deploy (exclude=_keep_generated_shippable(rsync_excludes) — only ever REMOVES excludes), aggregate_flow's out/_per_task_results/_per_task_traces sinks, combiner _final_reduce writing remote _aggregated/<run_id>/, and fingerprint_store/conformance_store rooting their ledgers under _aggregated/. Grep confirms no code path adds an `_aggregated` exclude; not covered by the architecture-review's transfer items (it only banks the ops/transfer→infra move) nor the fixed-bug train.
triage: Verified transport.py:107-118 PROTECTED_OUTPUT_DIRS = results/, _combiner/, logs/ only; _aggregated absent from all three exclude lists. Push-back + remote-clobber real. Architecture review only banks ops/transfer->infra move.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed _aggregated appears in none of DEFAULT_RSYNC_EXCLUDES / MANDATORY / PROTECTED_OUTPUT_DIRS (results,_combiner,logs) / PROTECTED_RUNTIME; the local aggregate tree (out=experiment_dir/_aggregated/<run_id>, incl. _per_task_results at line 402 and _fingerprints at fingerprint_store.py:164) sits under experiment_dir, and _push_and_deploy pushes local_path=experiment_dir with delete=True default while _keep_generated_shippable only removes excludes. So every re-submit ships the pulled per-task/fingerprint tree up and --delete clobbers the cluster-side _aggregated written by the final reduce. Transfer-waste + remote-clobber both real; medium.

title: A standing consent recorded AFTER the driver parked at the gated boundary is never consulted: run_tick's awaiting_decision path skips the overnight seam, so the consented S3 launch never happens and the night is silently lost
failure_scenario: Evening: canary finishes early and a block-drive tick parks the run at the S2→S3 gate (pending marker written, awaiting_since=T0). The human then says 'run it overnight' and the agent records a valid overnight-consent (caps, cmd_sha, wake armed — every gate passes). Every subsequent tick (status-watch terminal re-invoke, doctor, stop-guard completer, cron) reads the pending marker, finds no committed y targeting submit-s3, and returns awaiting_decision/exit 0 — _consume_overnight is only called at the _chain gated-successor park site (block_drive.py:798-809), i.e. only when the boundary is reached FRESH inside a tick that has a live consent already recorded. The parked-marker resume path never consults the consent, even though submit-s3's own gate (assert_greenlit_or_consented) would accept it if invoked. Result: main array never launches; morning brief shows a consent with consumed_count=0 and no explanation. The same race fires if the canary completes during the minutes the human is typing the consent.
fix_sketch: In run_tick, when plan_block_action returns awaiting_decision and the marker's resume_cursor.next_verb is a gated block (block_chain.is_gated), consult _consume_overnight(experiment_dir, run_id, next_verb) before returning; on consumed=True, treat it as an advance into next_verb using the marker's next_spec_hint (clear the marker, run the span, with the existing on_first_failure re-park). Add a test: pending marker at submit-s2→submit-s3 + live consent → tick advances and ledgers, instead of awaiting_decision.
evidence: Read run_tick end-to-end: the only consent consult is inside _chain's gated-successor branch (line 799); the resume/awaiting path (lines 541-567) returns without it. tests/ops/decision/test_overnight_wiring.py:291-341 covers only the in-chain seam (_chain called directly); tests/_kernel/lifecycle/test_block_drive.py:470 (test_run_tick_awaiting_when_pending_uncommitted) pins awaiting_decision with no consent present — the marker+consent combination is untested. assert_wake_armed/arm_consent_wake (overnight.py:193-235, 426-455) happily record a consent in this state, so nothing warns the user it can never be consumed.
triage: Consent recorded after park never consulted on the awaiting_decision resume path (only _chain gated-successor site consults it); novel scoping hole. Real lost night.

votes.reasoning: Confirmed run_tick's resume path (block_drive.py:500-536) computes committed_resolved via the boundary-scoped scan and, when None, plan_block_action returns awaiting_decision (235-244) and run_tick returns at 541 — never reaching the only _consume_overnight consult at the _chain gated-successor site (799). A grep found just three consent-consult sites; none run on marker-resume, and submit-s3's own gate never runs because the driver parks first. A consent recorded after the S2->S3 park is genuinely stranded. Fail-safe (parks, no wrong action), so I lower severity to medium.

grep shows the overnight consent is consumed at exactly three sites; run_tick returns for action=='awaiting_decision' at block_drive.py:556-567 before ever reaching _chain (:651) where the only driver-side _consume_overnight lives (:799), and submit-s3's gate never runs because the driver never advances into it. A consent recorded after the run has already parked at the gate is therefore consulted by nothing and the night is lost — a natural operator flow (see 'awaiting decision', say 'run it overnight').

title: Pending-decision marker is cleared before the resumed span runs and only re-parked on a clean non-zero exit — a crash/SIGKILL during the span or an exception from process spawn (fork exhaustion, OSError) permanently downgrades a human's rerun-nudge to a journal-derived advance that silently drops the edit
failure_scenario: Human nudges an S1-owned field, LLM redrafts, human commits y; run_tick routes 'rerun', calls clear_pending_decision, then runs the S1 span — which can take minutes (resolve + SSH). The machine reboots / the tick is OOM-killed mid-span, or subprocess.Popen inside _capture_via_select raises OSError (the documented fork-exhaustion night) — an exception path that bypasses on_first_failure (it only fires when _run_block_verb RETURNS an empty result; only TimeoutExpired is caught, block_drive.py:362-366). The marker, including the §4 input_spec diff base, is gone. The next tick takes the no-marker journal-derived path: the committed y's resolved.next_block names the parked successor → 'advance' straight into the gated block; the rerun that would have recomputed the current block's derived fields never happens, and edited fields not declared on the successor's spec model are silently filtered out (block_drive.py:609-613). The successor runs with pre-edit derived state — the human's correction vanishes without any error.
fix_sketch: Make the clear transactional with the first span: run the first resumed span BEFORE clearing (clear only after a non-empty result), or wrap the first span in try/finally so any exception path re-parks via the existing _repark_marker; at minimum catch OSError alongside TimeoutExpired in _run_block_verb and return ({}, code) so on_first_failure fires. A durable 'consuming' tombstone (marker moved aside, restored on next tick if no terminal appeared) would close the hard-crash window too.
evidence: Read run_tick lines 569-586 (clear before _chain), _chain lines 696-703 (on_first_failure only on empty result), _run_block_verb lines 359-366 (only TimeoutExpired caught; Popen in infra/remote.py:650 can raise OSError uncaught). tests/_kernel/lifecycle/test_block_drive.py:683 pins the re-park on a FAILED (non-zero exit) first span only; no test covers an exception or crash between clear and completion. The comment at block_drive.py:571-575 describes exactly this degradation as the thing the re-park exists to prevent — the guard cannot fire on the exception/crash legs.
triage: clear_pending_decision before span with re-park only on non-zero exit; crash/OSError legs bypass on_first_failure. Novel crash-window state loss. Not recorded fixed.

votes.reasoning: clear_pending_decision runs at block_drive.py:579 before the resumed span; on_first_failure re-park fires only when _run_block_verb RETURNS empty (700-702), and _run_block_verb catches only subprocess.TimeoutExpired (362-366) — a hard crash/OOM/SIGKILL mid-span or an OSError from spawning the capture subprocess propagates without re-park. The next tick's no-marker journal-derived path advances on the committed y into the gated successor, filtering out the edited S1-owned field (607-613), silently dropping the human's correction. The re-park guard genuinely cannot fire on the exception/crash legs.

title: Overnight-consent cmd_sha binding is ambiguous between two different identity tokens and never validated at record time: a consent bound to the §4 input-spec sha (which the substrate's own docs name, and which the parked marker displays) always refuses 'spec-changed' at consumption
failure_scenario: The agent records a run-scope consent. overnight.py's module docstring and assert_consent_hard_caps both say cmd_sha is 'the block-drive §4 input-spec identity ... the SAME token a pre-y is carried under' — that token is _spec_sha(spec), which is exactly what the parked marker's visible cmd_sha field carries (_park stamps cmd_sha=_spec_sha, block_drive.py:957). But both consumption sites compare against the run SIDECAR tree fingerprint (read_run_sidecar()['cmd_sha'] in _consume_overnight and submit_blocks._current_cmd_sha) — a completely different derivation (compute_run_id tree hash vs sha256 of the spec JSON). cmd_sha is deliberately never composed and never cross-checked at record time (compose_consent_defaults skips it; assert_consent_hard_caps only checks presence). An agent that follows the substrate's own docstring or copies the marker's cmd_sha produces a consent for which standing_consent_status returns 'spec-changed' at every boundary → S3 parks all night with reason '(no live standing consent to auto-advance overnight: spec-changed)' buried in a brief nobody is awake to read. All wiring tests seed consent and consumption with the same literal constant, so the cross-derivation mismatch is never exercised.
fix_sketch: Validate at record time: for a run scope, _assert_overnight_consent_authorship (or compose_overnight_consent) should compare resolved.cmd_sha against read_run_sidecar(scope_id)['cmd_sha'] (and for campaign scope against _campaign_spec_identity(manifest)) and refuse with a message naming the expected token — or better, compose it from the sidecar (it is code-derivable, unlike the docstring's claim; the trust boundary is the SCOPE, not the sha value). Fix the overnight.py/notebook-audit.md wording to name the sidecar tree fingerprint. Add a test recording a consent with the marker's _spec_sha and asserting the record-time gate refuses.
evidence: Read all three token sources: _consume_overnight (sidecar sha, block_drive.py:884-894), submit_blocks._current_cmd_sha (sidecar sha, submit_blocks.py:73-86, used at 825), _park (marker cmd_sha = _spec_sha, block_drive.py:957), and the contradictory prose (overnight.py:17-21 and 249-253 say '§4 input-spec identity'; docs/design/notebook-audit.md line ~870 cites block_drive._spec_sha while line ~905 says 'keyed on the run's sidecar cmd_sha'). tests/ops/decision/test_overnight_wiring.py uses one shared _CMD_SHA constant on both sides, so no test pins that a real recording flow binds the token consumption actually compares.
triage: cmd_sha token mismatch: _park stamps _spec_sha vs consumption comparing sidecar tree fingerprint; never validated at record time. Novel; not the G3 bound-capture item.

votes.reasoning: _park stamps the marker cmd_sha as _spec_sha(spec) (SHA over spec JSON, 99-108/957), but both consumption sites compare the sidecar cmd_sha = compute_cmd_sha over materialized task kwargs (_consume_overnight:885, submit_blocks._current_cmd_sha:73-87) — provably different derivations. overnight.py's docstrings (17-21, 250) and the visible marker both point at the §4 spec-sha token, and the record-time authorship gate (journal.py:3676-3711) only checks internal consistency + presence, never against the sidecar. Wiring tests seed the matching token so the cross-derivation refusal is never exercised. Failure hinges on which token the agent supplies, but the authoritative guidance and marker both mislead toward the always-refusing one.

title: Consent budget/walltime caps are unenforceable for the boundaries they exist to bound: no consumption site records spent_budget/spent_walltime, so consumed_spend meters ~0 forever, and cross-night idempotent re-consumption is authorized with zero morning-brief disclosure
failure_scenario: assert_consent_hard_caps REFUSES any consent lacking budget_cap/walltime_cap ('a consent that caps neither spend nor walltime accepts unbounded overnight fallout'), and standing_consent_status has over-budget-cap/over-walltime-cap refusal legs fed by consumed_spend. But consumed_spend only sums detail.spent_budget/spent_walltime from ledger lines, and NO consumption writer supplies them: the driver's _consume_overnight passes detail=None, submit-s3's gate passes no detail, campaign_watch passes {anomaly, reason} — grep shows zero writers of spent_* anywhere in src outside overnight.py itself (the §8 'B heal' writers do not exist yet). So the caps the human is forced to declare are compared against 0.0 every night: an S3 main-array launch burning 10x the walltime_cap is still auto-advanced. Separately, _already_consumed returns ConsumptionOutcome(True, decision, line=None) when the same (boundary, cmd_sha) was ledgered on a PREVIOUS night under an earlier consent — a later night's re-crossing under a fresh consent is authorized with no new ledger line, so that night's morning brief shows consumed_count=0 for an advance that actually happened.
fix_sketch: Feed the meter at the sites that know the cost: _consume_overnight / assert_greenlit_or_consented should stamp detail.spent_walltime from the run's requested walltime × task count (sidecar resources) and detail.spent_budget from the same source campaign_budget's compute_spend uses; campaign_watch should pass the campaign's realised spend (campaign_budget's 'spent'). Scope _already_consumed's idempotency key to the CURRENT consent (e.g. include the consent record's ts in the key, or only dedup lines newer than the consent's own timestamp) so each night's consumption is ledgered. Add a test where a consent with walltime_cap below the run's requested walltime refuses the S3 auto-advance.
evidence: Read consumed_spend (overnight.py:692-720 — only detail.spent_* counted), consume_boundary_under_consent (885-914 — meter defaults, stamped_detail={'cmd_sha', **detail}), the three real callers (driver block_drive.py:888-894 detail=None; gate block_gate.py:189-195 no detail; watch blocks.py:433-441 no spend), and _already_consumed (901-904 returns line=None while still authorizing). grep 'spent_budget|spent_walltime' across src finds no writer outside overnight.py. Per the repo's own engineering rule ('verify a guard can actually fire'), the over-*-cap legs can only fire from heal-attempt lines that nothing currently writes with a cost; tests (test_over_budget_cap_parks) pass spent_budget explicitly, never through the metered path.
triage: Consent caps compared against consumed_spend that no consumption site feeds (spent_* never written); guard-cannot-fire per repo's own rule. Novel, not recorded fixed.

votes.reasoning: grep across src confirms no writer sets detail.spent_budget/spent_walltime outside overnight.py; the three real callers pass detail=None (block_drive:888), no detail (block_gate:189-195), and {anomaly,reason} (blocks.py:433-441). consumed_spend (692-720) therefore always meters ~0, so standing_consent_status's over-budget/over-walltime legs (555-559) can never fire despite assert_consent_hard_caps forcing a cap — a guard that cannot fire per the repo's own rule. _already_consumed (818-839) also authorizes cross-night re-crossings with line=None (no disclosure). The run-scope '10x walltime' example is weaker (a single s3 boundary is checked at spend=0 regardless), but the campaign repeated-consumption cap and the disclosure gap are genuinely broken.

title: Partial reproduction (HPC_TASK_INCLUDE) never settles: non-selected tasks write no result, no marker, and no consumer of task_sample shrinks the expected set — every partial repro watch terminates ABANDONED or TIMEOUT
failure_scenario: User reproduces 10 of 1000 tasks via reproduce-run's task_sample. By design the FULL task shape is kept (task_count=1000 on the sidecar/run record) and selection rides HPC_TASK_INCLUDE; a non-selected task exits 0 in the dispatcher BEFORE the WIP/announce bookkeeping (dispatch.py:774), writing no result file and no terminal marker. The repro run is watched by the standard monitor with total_tasks=1000: on the walk path the 990 non-selected tasks read scheduler-COMPLETED-but-no-result = 'unknown' forever, so after the unknown streak the run is classified terminal ABANDONED ('unknown-exhausted' — the vanished-workdir anomaly); on the announce-census path they read missing→pending forever and the watch rides the full wall-clock budget to TIMEOUT. Either way a perfectly healthy partial reproduction ends in an anomaly terminal, hours late, and any automation keyed on COMPLETE (harvest-on-terminal transition, fingerprint minting flow) runs only via the cap-overrun/anomaly path. grep shows task_sample is consumed ONLY by verify_reproduction (comparison), never by status/classify/monitor.
fix_sketch: Thread the selected set into lifecycle accounting: persist task_sample where the monitor can see it (it is already in sidecar extra['task_sample']) and (a) have _build_per_task_dict_from_sidecar / the run record expose an effective_total = len(task_sample) when present, or (b) have the dispatcher write a 'skipped'/complete announce marker for non-selected indices (cheap — it already exits 0 for them) so both census and walk counts close. Add a test: monitor of a run with task_sample={0,1} out of 4 settles COMPLETE when the 2 selected tasks finish.
evidence: Read reproduce_run.py (selection 'threads through TWO seams' — job env + sidecar extra, explicitly for verify-reproduction only), dispatch.py include-skip (exits before result/announce writes; comment says 'the reduce never sees a spurious row' but nothing about status), classify.unresolved_unknown (complete<total & running==0 & pending==0 & failed==0 & unknown>0 → abandoned after streak), census mapping missing→pending. tests: tests/ops/submit/test_reproduce_run.py and test_dispatch.py cover the skip and spec; tests/ops/aggregate/test_verify_reproduction.py covers comparison — no test runs the monitor against a partial reproduction. Not in refuted/fixed/banked docs.
triage: HPC_TASK_INCLUDE partial repro never settles; task_sample not consumed by monitor/classify. Novel lifecycle defect, not in refuted/fixed/banked.

votes.reasoning: dispatch.py:769-774 exits 0 before any result/announce write for non-selected indices; grep confirms task_sample is consumed only by reproduce_run/verify_reproduction and nothing shrinks the monitor's total_tasks, which stays at the full task_count. So a partial repro's census leg reads missing>0->pending forever (->TIMEOUT) or the walk reads unknown->ABANDONED; it can never settle COMPLETE. Structurally verified.

title: deploy_runtime rewrites live framework files with rsync --inplace (tar-overwrite fallback): a mid-campaign submit tears _hpc_dispatch.py/preambles under running arrays, and the retry wrapper converts the torn-file window into terminal .hpc_failed markers
failure_scenario: Campaign A's 500-task array is mid-flight in remote_path when the user submits run B from the same experiment after a `pip install -U hpc-agent` (or any framework-file change) — deploy_runtime's cache sees changed shas and re-ships .hpc/_hpc_dispatch.py and the preambles with `rsync --inplace`, which mutates the destination file bytes in place (chosen deliberately for delta transfer). Any of A's tasks that starts during the transfer (or during an rsync stall on a flaky WAN link) execs a torn dispatcher: SyntaxError, exit 1. hpc_run_with_retry retries 3x within ~6s (still inside the torn window on a slow link), then writes the cross-invocation terminal marker `$RESULT_DIR/.hpc_failed/<run>.<task>.failed` — after which even a scheduler relaunch of that task refuses to run ('already marked terminally failed') until a deliberate resubmit clears it. Net: healthy tasks of an unrelated in-flight run become permanently failed with a misleading SyntaxError forensic trail.
fix_sketch: Make the deploy write-then-rename: rsync into a staging dir on the cluster (or use rsync's default temp-file+rename by DROPPING --inplace — the delta benefit is marginal for <100KB python files) and keep --inplace only for the tar fallback if it must, or tar-extract with --unlink-first so readers keep the old inode. Alternatively gate re-shipping of dispatch/preamble files when the journal shows live runs in the same remote_path, with a disclosed warning.

The G12 atomic-write generator in the fix train covered local durable JSON writes (infra/io.atomic_write_json) — the remote deploy transport is not in its member list, the bug-sweep transport findings (#9/#10/#11 win32, #36 filter) are different defects, and no test exercises a deploy concurrent with a running array.

Both fixed by making the deploy atomic; not a single defect.
