The transient torn-read is readily reachable; the permanent-marker wedge is the narrower flaky-WAN-stall tail, consistent with the claimed medium severity.

title: Terminal-FAILED tick crashes the whole watch when pack declarations are stale: resolve_failure_patterns raises SpecInvalid (deliberately loud) inside monitor_flow's unguarded FAILED branch, even for runs with auto-recover opt-in OFF, leaving the run non-terminal
failure_scenario: A repo opted into a domain pack (interview.json 'packs' entry — the quant pack just shipped tier-1 at HEAD) whose bind later goes stale: manifest moved/edited (sha mismatch), receipt superseded with no current bind, or a malformed entry. Weeks later a run fails mid-campaign. monitor_flow's FAILED tick calls maybe_resolve_and_recover unconditionally (:855 — 'behavior-neutral until a run opts in' only covers side effects); with any fetched failure cluster it calls resolve_failure_patterns(experiment_dir) (:379), which raises errors.SpecInvalid by design ('loud, never a silent pass', pack_declarations.py:319-324). maybe_resolve_and_recover guards only pydantic ValidationError (:416); monitor_flow's FAILED branch has no try/except — the SpecInvalid propagates, the multi-hour detached watch dies with an exception BEFORE mark_terminal runs, the finally-harvest clean-skips (run not terminal), and the run is stranded in-flight. Every re-watch crashes the same way at the same tick — the recovery path is wedged by unrelated pack state at exactly the moment failure classification is needed.
fix_sketch: Wrap the pack-declaration resolution at resolve_and_recover_flow.py:379 in try/except HpcError and degrade to failure_patterns=[] with the reason surfaced on the outcome (the features glue only COUNTS pattern hits — losing them degrades evidence, not correctness), or catch HpcError around the whole maybe_resolve_and_recover call in monitor_flow's FAILED branch and fall through to the plain FAILED surface with the error appended to esc_reason. The pack staleness itself is already loud on submit paths; the monitor tick is the wrong place to enforce it.
evidence: Read maybe_resolve_and_recover (only ValidationError and fetch errors guarded; :379 bare), _resolve_packs (four distinct SpecInvalid raise sites incl. 'no CURRENT bind'), monitor_flow FAILED branch (no exception handling from :785 to :943). tests/ops/monitor/test_flow_resolve_and_recover.py repos have no pack opt-in so the raise path is untested; tests/state pack tests pin the raises as intended, but no test pins what the monitor does when they fire. Distinct from the fixed bug-sweep #2 (ValidationError vocabulary gap) — that fix's defense-in-depth explicitly catches only ValidationError.
triage: resolve_and_recover_flow.py:379 raises SpecInvalid by design; monitor_flow FAILED branch is unguarded. Distinct from fixed bug #2 (which catches only ValidationError). Not banked.

votes.reasoning: maybe_resolve_and_recover is called unconditionally at monitor_flow:855 and calls resolve_failure_patterns at :379 with no guard (its only try/except at :410-432 catches pydantic ValidationError around build_failure_features). _resolve_packs raises SpecInvalid (pack_declarations.py:271/299/304/320) whenever a repo opted into a pack whose bind is stale/malformed, while returning [] for the common non-opted-in case. The FAILED branch (785-943) has no enclosing try, and the outer finally only suppresses harvest exceptions and re-raises, so the SpecInvalid kills the detached watch before mark_terminal, stranding the run non-terminal. Reachability is gated on a (new, tier-1) pack opt-in going stale — narrow but real and accurately described.

title: Auto-recover's resubmit and cap-counter journal write are unguarded on the terminal-FAILED tick: an SSH blip during resubmit_flow (or an OSError persisting auto_recover_count) kills the watch after work was already put on the cluster, and the lost bump loosens the cap — the exact hazard its auto-resume twin documents and guards
failure_scenario: Run with auto_recover_on_failure=True fails with system_oom at 2am; the resolver fires resubmit_flow with mem*1.5. The qsub-over-ssh hits a transient SshUnreachable/RemoteCommandFailed (flaky WAN at exactly the moment the monitor already survived a dozen tolerated poll blips) -> the exception propagates from resolve_and_recover_flow.py:579 through the unguarded FAILED branch, killing the detached watch; the run stays non-terminal. Worse: if the resubmit SUCCEEDED but the journal write at :598 fails (NFS/homedir hiccup), the exception kills the watch AND the auto_recover_count bump is lost — the next watch reads the stale count and fires an extra resubmit PAST the intended cap. auto_resume_flow.py:227-248 fixed this exact failure mode for its counter ('an uncaught write failure here would (a) crash the monitor's terminal-FAILED tick outright and (b) ... LOOSENS the cap') but the newer resolve-and-recover copy shipped without the guard, and in BOTH composites the resubmit call itself (auto_resume_flow.py:202, resolve_and_recover_flow.py:579) is bare while every status-poll fault in the same loop is elaborately tolerated.
fix_sketch: Mirror the auto-resume counter guard at resolve_and_recover_flow.py:598 (catch HpcError/OSError, log loudly, keep the in-memory bump). Wrap both composites' resubmit(...) calls in try/except HpcError/OSError and convert the failure into a held/verdict outcome (reason='resubmit failed: ...') so the monitor falls through to the normal FAILED surface instead of dying — one parked cluster must not kill the watch, per the module's own never-blocks policy.
evidence: Read both composites side by side; the fail-closed comment block exists only in auto_resume_flow. monitor_flow's FAILED branch has no enclosing try (the poll-tolerance handling at :495-639 covers only record_status). resubmit_flow 'raises typed errors' by contract (recover_flow.py:13) including SpecInvalid/Preempted/SSH errors after validation. tests inject a fake resubmit that never raises (test_flow_resolve_and_recover.py, test_flow_auto_resume.py) — the raise path is unpinned.
triage: Twin-drift: G15/#69 guard landed only in auto_resume_flow; resolve_and_recover's resubmit+counter (resolve_and_recover_flow.py:579-598) shipped without it. New site, not a banked proposal.

votes.reasoning: Read both composites: auto_resume_flow wraps its counter write in try/except HpcError/OSError with a fail-closed in-memory bump (234-248), but resolve_and_recover_flow:596-599 writes auto_recover_count with no guard, and both resubmit(...) calls (auto_resume:202, resolve_and_recover:579) are bare. An SSH fault in resubmit or an OSError persisting the counter propagates through the unguarded FAILED branch and finally-re-raise, killing the watch non-terminal; a resubmit-succeeded-but-write-failed leaves the bump lost so the next watch reads a stale count and loosens the cap — exactly the twin hazard auto_resume documents and guards. Requires auto_recover opt-in plus a transient fault at the resubmit/write instant.

title: consecutive_env_polls is never reset on a successful poll, so NON-consecutive rc-126/127 blips across a long watch accumulate to the threshold and kill a healthy monitor with a spurious 'reporter UNREACHABLE' TIMEOUT
failure_scenario: Pre-announce (walk-leg) watch over a multi-hour run on a cluster where the login-node conda env is occasionally mid-update or NFS lag makes the interpreter momentarily unresolvable: poll 12 fails rc=127, polls 13-40 succeed cleanly, poll 41 fails rc=127, polls 42-90 succeed, poll 91 fails rc=127 -> consecutive_env_polls reaches 3 (it is only incremented at :503, reset at :505 on a non-env failure and at :585 on an ssh fault — the successful-poll path from :640 never touches it) -> the monitor escalates to lifecycle TIMEOUT with 'status reporter UNREACHABLE — 3 consecutive deterministic failures' (:542) even though the reporter worked for the intervening hours. The watch exits mid-experiment with a diagnosis ('fix the cluster env then re-watch') that is wrong, and the campaign driver treats the run as needing a decision.
fix_sketch: Add `consecutive_env_polls = 0` after a successful record_status (immediately after the try block, e.g. before line 640's last_status assignment), matching the variable's name and the escalation message's 'consecutive' claim; also reset it on the census leg's success for symmetry.
evidence: Grep of all 7 occurrences confirms no reset on success. tests/ops/monitor/test_flow_poll_tolerance.py:142 pins the every-poll-fails case (asserts ticks == threshold) and the single-transient case, but no test interleaves env failures with successful polls — the intent ('fails EVERY poll identically', :153-157 comment) is unpinned against this counterexample. rc-127 as transient is realistic: ssh_status_report's import-probe deliberately converts ANY momentary import failure to exit 127 (cluster_status.py:152-162).
triage: Grep confirms consecutive_env_polls reset only at init/505/585, never on a successful poll — non-consecutive rc-127 blips accumulate to threshold. Not banked.

votes.reasoning: Grep confirms consecutive_env_polls is reset only at init (419), on a non-env RemoteCommandFailed/OSError (505), and on an ssh fault (585) — never on the successful-poll path (640+). cluster_status.py:162 routes ANY failure of the `import hpc_agent` probe to `exit 127`, so a momentary import failure (NFS/Lustre lag, conda env mid-update) yields a transient rc-127; three such non-consecutive blips across a walk-leg watch accumulate to threshold=3 and fire a spurious reporter-UNREACHABLE TIMEOUT despite the reporter working in between. Real defect (counter misnamed vs. the escalation's 'consecutive' claim); impact is a recoverable false escalation (guaranteed harvest still runs), and the trigger is plausible though modest in frequency and scoped to the pre-announce/fallback walk leg.

title: auto_combine_waves silently no-ops on the announce census leg: census last_status never carries a 'waves' block, so announce-era runs never combine waves mid-flight, failed_waves stays empty, and the terminal harvest (ensure_all_combined=False) has nothing combined to harvest
failure_scenario: A wave-mapped array run with auto_combine_waves=true (the documented submit-flow -> monitor-flow campaign composition): as soon as the dispatcher writes its first announce marker, every tick takes the census leg, whose last_status is built at monitor_flow.py:219-238 with only the 5 counts + task_announcements — no 'waves' block (only the walk reporter emits one, status.py:199-200). _newly_complete_waves bails at waves.py:34-36 on the missing block, so combine_wave never fires for ANY wave during the whole run. At terminal, harvest_on_terminal aggregates with ensure_all_combined=False ('harvest whatever already combined') — nothing was — so the guaranteed harvest yields an empty metrics envelope, MonitorFlowResult.combined_waves is empty and failed_waves can never populate, and the COMPLETE-with-failed-waves escalation (:769-773) is dead. A campaign step that keys the next iteration off combined waves or the failure ledger sees a clean-but-empty result and either stalls or re-does all combining serially at aggregate time (one big cluster-side burst instead of incremental combines).
fix_sketch: On the census leg, when auto_combine_waves && wave_map, derive per-wave completeness from the announce markers themselves (the marker filenames carry task ids — extend read_announcements to optionally return the id lists, or run the existing wave-listing probe) OR fall back to the walk on ticks where wave bookkeeping is due. At minimum, log the degrade once (the DISCLOSURE-OR-REFUSAL bar this same file applies to the walk fallback) instead of silently dropping a spec'd behavior.
evidence: Read the census-leg last_status construction (no waves key), _newly_complete_waves' isinstance-dict gate, and harvest_guard._default_aggregate's ensure_all_combined=False. tests/ops/monitor/test_flow_announce.py never sets auto_combine_waves with a wave_map; the combine tests all inject walk-leg statuses carrying 'waves'. Untested interaction; the Phase-2 drift-log entry in docs/design/crash-only-monitoring.md discusses only the lifecycle verdict, not wave combining.
triage: Confirmed census last_status (monitor_flow.py:219-238) carries no 'waves' block, so auto_combine_waves silently no-ops on announce-era runs. Not banked.

votes.reasoning: The census last_status (monitor_flow.py:219-238) carries only the 5 counts + task_announcements and no `waves` key; _newly_complete_waves returns [] on a missing/non-dict waves block (waves.py:34-36), and only the walk-leg reporter emits `waves` (status.py:199-200). Since the census leg takes over as soon as the dispatcher writes its first marker, auto_combine_waves silently no-ops for the rest of any announce-era wave-mapped run, so combined_waves/failed_waves never populate and the guaranteed terminal harvest runs aggregate_flow with ensure_all_combined=False (harvest_guard.py:308) -> empty envelope. A genuine silent regression of a spec'd behavior; data is not permanently lost because the user-driven aggregate-flow can re-combine, so medium impact is appropriate.

title: `hpc-agent setup --cluster <name>` exits 0 with ok:true on a red probe, and the '24h cache marker' it writes on green is dead — no code or skill ever reads preflight-<cluster>.json, contradicting README's 'Step 6b gate'
failure_scenario: A user follows README quickstart: `hpc-agent setup --cluster hofman2` (typo) or runs it while their VPN is down. _setup_handler emits {ok: true, idempotent: true} and returns EXIT_OK regardless of preflight['all_ok']; a scripted bootstrap (or a user glancing at the exit code) proceeds believing setup succeeded — README explicitly says 'runtime workflows assume setup succeeded.' Separately, the marker written on green (write_preflight_marker) has no consumer: grep shows the only references are the writer, the layout path helper, and tests of the writer — no 'Step 6b' exists in submit-hpc.md or the hpc-submit SKILL, no TTL check exists anywhere, and layout.py:187-191's claim that 'hpc-submit's pre-flight gate reads it' and that 'both skills' markdown document the literal' is false at HEAD. So setup's cluster probe provides zero durable assurance, while README sells it as the one-time-per-machine environment gate.
fix_sketch: In _setup_handler (and the setup() primitive), map preflight['all_ok']=False to a non-zero exit (EXIT_CLUSTER_ERROR) with the failing checks in the envelope, mirroring the documented check-preflight contract. Then either wire a real marker consumer (e.g. submit_preflight skips the ssh probe arm when a <24h marker exists — the probe_cache already implements the freshness pattern) or delete write_preflight_marker + the README/layout prose so setup stops asserting a gate that does not exist.
evidence: Read cli/setup.py handlers (unconditional _emit({'ok': True,...}); return EXIT_OK), write_preflight_marker, layout.preflight_marker docstring, README lines 5/17/24/95. Grepped the whole src tree + slash_commands for readers of preflight_marker / 'preflight-<cluster>' / 'Step 6b': only writer-side hits. tests/cli/test_setup.py:48-49,81-99 pins rc==0 even for the red-probe case (asserts only the marker is absent), so the exit-0-on-red behavior is test-pinned but the marker's consumption is pinned nowhere — it can't be, there is none.
triage: setup --cluster exits 0 on a red probe and write_preflight_marker has no consumer (grep in finding); analogous to but distinct from F29. Not banked.

related.note: Same failure class (a preflight-bearing CLI returns exit 0/ok:true despite all_ok=False) via different handlers: F29 = generic dispatch_primitive _ok() wrapper for the `preflight` verb; F31 = setup.py's own _setup_handler for `setup` (plus an independent dead-preflight-marker defect). Fixing one does not fix the other.

votes.reasoning: _setup_handler (cli/setup.py:87-116) and the setup primitive func (197-239) call check_preflight but emit {ok:true, idempotent:true} / return EXIT_OK with no branch on preflight['all_ok'] — a red probe still exits 0. Grep across src/ and slash_commands confirms preflight_marker()/write_preflight_marker has only writer-side and test references; no runtime consumer, no TTL check, and no 'Step 6b' string exists in the hpc-submit SKILL or submit-hpc command. README (lines 5,24,96) and layout.py:187-191 both assert a Step 6b gate that reads the marker — that gate does not exist at HEAD. Both halves hold.

title: clusters.yaml entries are never schema-validated on any runtime path — validate_clusters_config has zero callers and ClusterConfig uses extra='ignore', so a misspelled key silently disables its feature and a wrong-typed value only explodes deep in a submit
failure_scenario: A user customizing ~/.hpc-agent/clusters.yaml writes `conda_env:` instead of `conda_envs:` (or `nfs-data-dir`, `max_walltime: 24h`). load_clusters_config returns the raw yaml dict; check-preflight's clusters_yaml_parses check only proves the YAML parses; validate_clusters_config — the 'opt-in strong contract' — is called from nowhere in src (grep: definition only), and ClusterConfig's extra='ignore' means every consumer that DOES model_validate (resolve_ssh_target, scaffold_spec, revise_resolved) silently drops the unknown key. Result: the job preamble omits `conda activate`, the canary (or worse, on a canary-skipped resubmit, the whole array) dies cluster-side with rc 127 / 'No module named ...', hours after the typo, with nothing pointing at clusters.yaml. Wrong-typed values (e.g. a string max_walltime_sec) surface as SpecInvalid from a typed getter mid-resolve instead of at config load — despite the module docstring promising 'fails loudly at load time'.
fix_sketch: Call validate_clusters_config from check_preflight (right after the parse check, per-entry, reporting a `cluster_config_valid` check) and from load_clusters_config's user-file/env-file branches (packaged default exempt). To catch misspelled keys, add a warn-on-unknown-keys pass comparing each entry's keys against _allowed_cluster_keys() — extra='ignore' can stay for forward-compat, but the preflight check should name near-miss keys (difflib against the allowlist).
evidence: Grepped validate_clusters_config across src: only the definition at clusters.py:806. Read load_clusters_config (raw yaml.safe_load, no per-entry validation) and check_preflight (parse-only check + placeholder scan). Read ClusterConfig model_config extra='ignore' with the comment acknowledging unknown fields pass silently. The docstring at clusters.py:3-8 ('fails loudly at load time') is contradicted by the load path. tests/infra/test_clusters.py exercises the typed getters but nothing pins any runtime caller of validate_clusters_config.
triage: Grep confirms validate_clusters_config (clusters.py:806) has zero callers; ClusterConfig extra='ignore'. Config typos fail late. Not banked.

related.note: Both are clusters.yaml malformed-entry robustness gaps surfacing in check_preflight: F32 = no schema validation anywhere (validate_clusters_config has zero callers) so typos silently disable features; F33 = a None/empty entry crashes preflight with an internal AttributeError envelope. Distinct mechanisms, adjacent fix site.

votes.reasoning: grep confirms validate_clusters_config (clusters.py:806) has zero callers in src/ or tests. ClusterConfig sets model_config = ConfigDict(extra='ignore') (line 63), and load_clusters_config just returns yaml.safe_load with no per-entry validation (351-381), so a misspelled key like conda_env is silently dropped and check-preflight only proves the YAML parses. The 'fails loudly at load time' module docstring is contradicted — the typed getters raise only when called mid-resolve. The worst-case 'dooms the array' is partly mitigated by the first-submit canary catching rc-127, but the silent feature-disable and confusing late failure with no pointer to clusters.yaml are real; medium stands.

title: Federated SLURM: sbatch routes jobs with --clusters=<X> but squeue/scancel liveness, state, and cancel builders never emit -M <X> — jobs are invisible to every monitor query and un-killable, with verify_submitted's missing-doesn't-fail-gate making it silent
failure_scenario: User sets `slurm_cluster` (schema: 'cluster name for federated SLURM installations'). Submit emits `sbatch --clusters=gpu ...` — the job lives on cluster 'gpu'. build_alive_check_cmd/build_scheduler_state_cmd/build_cancel_cmd are classmethods taking only job_ids, so reconcile/kill/verify run plain `squeue -j <ids>` / `scancel <ids>` against the login node's default cluster: squeue prints nothing (ack present, ran_ok True) -> all jobs read gone immediately after a successful submit; verify_submitted reports them 'missing', which by pinned design does NOT fail the gate (bug-sweep refuted item on verify_submitted.py:134), so the pipeline proceeds; reconcile marks the healthy run abandoned; kill's scancel is a no-op yet verification confirms everything gone. Meanwhile query_sacct DOES thread cluster (`--clusters=` insert, query.py:158-159, _engine.py:993), proving the flag is known to be required — only the liveness/kill half lost it.
fix_sketch: Give the three builders an optional `cluster: str | None = None` kwarg emitting `-M {cluster}` for the slurm family (squeue/scancel both accept -M), and thread the value from the call sites that already hold it: reconcile has `getattr(record, 'cluster', None)` in hand at reconcile.py:812, kill can read it off the same RunRecord, verify_submitted off the spec's slurm_cluster. Default None keeps every existing command byte-identical.
evidence: Read _build_slurm_command:571-572 (`--clusters=` on submit), the three classmethod builders (no cluster parameter exists in their signatures), reconcile._ssh_alive_job_ids:140-142 and kill._attempt_backend_cancel:61-64 (call with job_ids only), query.py:158-159 (sacct half threads cluster). schemas/*.json confirm slurm_cluster is a supported user-facing config. No test covers alive-check/cancel with a cluster set (grepped tests for '-M'/'--clusters' in alive/cancel contexts). Not in the fixed/refuted/architecture-review lists (grepped squeue/scancel/federat).
triage: Confirmed the three builders (e.g. build_cancel_cmd) take only job_ids with no cluster kwarg, so squeue/scancel omit -M while sbatch emits --clusters. verify_submitted's missing-doesn't-fail is refuted but the -M gap is a distinct new defect. Not banked.

related.note: Same compounding as F35/F36 for federated SLURM: F37 (alive/cancel builders omit -M <cluster>) yields empty squeue output that F35's rc-blind ran_ok=True reads as 'gone', settling a healthy run abandoned and false-confirming kills.

votes.reasoning: build_alive_check_cmd/build_scheduler_state_cmd/build_cancel_cmd are @classmethods taking only job_ids (no cluster kwarg, so structurally cannot emit -M), while _build_slurm_command:571 emits --clusters= and query.py:158 threads --clusters= into sacct — a real, self-inconsistent asymmetry. In SLURM multi-cluster mode (what --clusters= actually implements) squeue/scancel without -M query only the default cluster and miss a routed job, compounding with F35. Reachability and impact are gated on the slurm_cluster config being set to a non-default cluster in a genuinely multi-cluster (vs auto-federation) deployment, where squeue's default federation view could otherwise find the job — hence medium, not high.

sbatch emits --clusters={self.cluster} (_engine.py:571-572) but build_alive_check_cmd/build_scheduler_state_cmd/build_cancel_cmd are classmethods with no cluster kwarg (:687,743,763), so squeue/scancel run against the login node's default cluster with no -M; query_sacct threads --clusters (:158-159), proving the flag is required and only the liveness/kill half dropped it. For a slurm_cluster-configured (multi-cluster) run, squeue returns empty-with-ack so the run settles abandoned and scancel silently no-ops. Real but scoped to the federated/multi-cluster config.

title: TORQUE completed jobs linger in live qstat as state 'C' for the keep_completed window but are counted alive and classified RUNNING — kill verification reports 'still alive' after a successful qdel, and finished runs can't settle until the window expires
failure_scenario: User kills a runaway array on a TORQUE cluster: qdel succeeds, TORQUE moves the job to state 'C' and keeps it in `qstat -t <ids>` output for keep_completed (300s default, sites configure minutes-hours). parse_alive_output counts any digit-led row as alive regardless of the state column, so kill's verification reports every job still_alive -> '<N> requested, 0 confirmed gone', settled=False; the user re-runs kill and gets the same answer, escalating (re-qdel, support ticket) against jobs that are already dead. Same window: batch_status maps 'C' (not in {Q,W,M,T}, not H/S/U) to RUNNING, so a normally-completed wave shows RUNNING and reconcile can't settle from queue absence until the record ages out. The engine's classify comment (:876-878) asserts 'Finished tokens (F/C/X) don't appear in the live qstat listing' — query.py:423-425 states the opposite for TORQUE ('retains a C record in plain qstat for a grace window').
fix_sketch: In the sge/pbs branch of parse_alive_output, when the family is pbspro/torque read cols[4] and skip rows whose state is in {'C','F','X'} (columns are already split; the brief format's state is index 4, exactly what parse_scheduler_states assumes). In classify_scheduler_state / _alive_task_status pbs branches, map 'C'/'F'/'X' to a terminal bucket instead of alive/RUNNING (batch_status can map them to COMPLETE-equivalent or simply omit, matching the 'absent = caller decides' contract). Fix the stale comment at _engine.py:876-878.
evidence: Read parse_alive_output (:723-740 — no state inspection), classify_scheduler_state pbs branch (:874-883 — only H/S/U special-cased, comment claims C never appears live), _alive_task_status (:939-944 — C falls to RUNNING), and query_pbs's contradicting docstring (query.py:423-425). tests/infra/backends/test_pbs.py's state table (lines 115-124) has no 'C' case — behavior untested. kill.py:137-146 shows still_alive blocks confirmed_gone and settle. Not covered by fixed bug #5 (rc semantics), the refuted list, or the architecture review.
triage: Confirmed parse_alive_output:737 checks only col-0 job id with no state-column inspection, counting a TORQUE 'C' row as alive; contradicts query_pbs docstring. Not in fixed/refuted.

votes.reasoning: parse_alive_output (_engine.py:730-739) keys only on a digit-led col-0 id with no state-column inspection, so a TORQUE 'C' row counts as alive; classify_scheduler_state (874-883) and _alive_task_status (939-944) map 'C' to alive/RUNNING. query.py:422 docstring states TORQUE retains a 'C' record in live qstat for a keep_completed grace window, directly contradicting the _engine.py:876-878 comment. Result: kill verification reports 'still alive' after a successful qdel and reconcile can't settle until the window ages out. Config-gated on keep_completed and TORQUE-only, so medium is right.

title: Canary TTL cache is keyed on parameter-only cmd_sha in a GLOBAL cross-experiment file, so a run with identical swept kwargs but different code/executor/repo silently skips its canary AND satisfies submit-s3's predecessor gate
failure_scenario: cmd_sha is PURE parameter identity (state/run_sha.py:43-76: only the materialized resolve(i) kwargs are hashed — no executor, no tasks_py_sha, no experiment identity), and the cache file is ~/.claude/hpc/_canary_cache.json at the journal-home ROOT, shared across every repo on the machine. Key = cmd_sha|version|cluster. Concrete: user canaries train.py over sweep_grid(seed=range(10)) (canary green, cached, TTL 4h), then — following the tool's own code-drift advice (--invalidate-on-code-change, runs.py:876-916) — submits evaluate.py (or an edited train.py, or the same seed sweep from a DIFFERENT repo) with identical kwargs. cmd_sha is unchanged, so _should_run_canary hits the cache and skips the canary entirely, and _assert_canary_verified (the S3 'S2's canary verified this tree' predecessor gate) PASSES on the other run's entry. The never-boot-tested executor's full N-task array launches; a broken import/env kills every task — exactly the failure class the canary gate exists to bound to 1 task. The in-file CAUTION only discloses env drift, not the code/executor/cross-experiment hole.
fix_sketch: Fold code identity into the key: canary_cache_key(cmd_sha, version, cluster, tasks_py_sha, executor_sha) — both read sites (submit_flow._should_run_canary, submit_blocks._assert_canary_verified) and the record site (verify_canary) already hold the run sidecar or job_env, which carry executor + tasks_py_sha (sidecar fields; submit-flow can thread them via job_env like HPC_CMD_SHA). Alternatively scope the cache file per repo namespace (journal_dir(experiment_dir)/'_canary_cache.json') to kill the cross-repo collision, plus executor/tasks_py_sha in the key for the same-repo entry-point case. Old two-part entries already 'simply never match again' per the proving-run-#5 precedent (canary_cache.py:97-98), so widening the key is the established migration.
evidence: Read canary_cache.py end-to-end (_cache_path at homedir root, key at :88-99), all three consumers (submit_flow.py:283-298 skip, submit_blocks.py:277-313 S3 gate, verify_canary.py:1300-1310 record), and compute_cmd_sha's docstring confirming params-only hashing. grep of tests: tests/ops/test_submit_flow_canary_skip.py and test_canary_verify.py only exercise same-key hit/miss; no test covers a colliding cmd_sha from a different executor/experiment. Not mentioned in upstream-fixes-2026-07.md, the refuted list, or architecture-review-2026-07-13.md.
triage: Verified canary_cache_key at canary_cache.py:88-99 is `{cmd_sha}|{version}|{cluster}` with no executor/tasks_py_sha; _cache_path is homedir root (global). Defect present.

votes.reasoning: Read run_sha.py:43-84 (cmd_sha docstring explicitly excludes executor source, rendered command, and tasks.py bytes), canary_cache.py (key = `{cmd_sha}|{version}|{cluster}` at :88-99, cache file at _current_homedir()/_canary_cache.json — global across all repos), and both consumers: submit_flow._canary_decision:288-299 (skip on cache hit) and submit_blocks._assert_canary_verified:300-313 (S3 gate passes on any fresh entry). Two experiments/repos sweeping identical kwargs (e.g. seed=range(10)) on the same cluster/version within the 4h TTL collide on the key, so a never-boot-tested executor skips its canary AND satisfies the S3 predecessor gate; the in-file CAUTION only discloses env drift, not the code/cross-repo hole.

title: Crash between a run-record write and its index refresh leaves index.json permanently claiming in_flight for a terminal run — find_in_flight_runs returns the record it just loaded WITHOUT re-checking its status, and the mtime staleness check never fires again once any sibling write bumps the index
where: src/hpc_agent/state/index.py:146-164, src/hpc_agent/state/journal.py:105-127 (write then unlocked index refresh), src/hpc_agent/state/index.py:81-90
failure_scenario: mark_run/upsert_run write the run file, release the per-run lock, THEN call _refresh_index_entry (journal.py:110/126/410). A crash (SIGKILL, OOM, laptop lid) in that gap leaves run A terminal on disk but in_flight in index.json. Any LATER write to a sibling run B refreshes the index, making index.json's mtime newer than A's file — so _index_is_stale (strict '>' per-file-mtime vs index-mtime) returns False forever and the index is never rebuilt. find_in_flight_runs then returns A as in-flight even though the RunRecord it loaded at index.py:159 says complete (it never checks record.status). Verified by repro in the project venv: index {'A': in_flight} while A on disk is complete, and find_in_flight_runs returns ['B','A']. Consequences persist for the rest of the campaign: doctor/find_stalled_runs flags the finished run as a stalled driver (lapsed next_tick_due) and drafts re-arm proposals every time, the campaign loop counts a phantom live run, and reconcile paths re-probe a done run. Coarse-mtime filesystems (NFS 1s granularity) hit the same window without needing the crash.
fix_sketch: In find_in_flight_runs, filter on the record actually loaded: after load_run at index.py:159, skip records whose status != 'in_flight' (the data is already in hand — one line), and optionally self-heal by calling _refresh_index_entry for the mismatched run so the index converges. A directory-mtime check in _index_is_stale cannot close this (the file content changed, not the file set), so the record-level cross-check is the right guard.
evidence: Reproduced with a script against d731b12 in the project venv (uv run): wrote A terminal via _atomic_write_json without the index refresh (simulated crash), refreshed the index via B's upsert, find_in_flight_runs returned A while load_run(A).status == 'complete'. tests/state/test_session.py covers missing/stale-index rebuilds (test_find_in_flight_with_missing_index) but nothing pins the crash-window/terminal-record-returned-as-in-flight case.
triage: Verified index.py:155-162 find_in_flight_runs loads record then appends WITHOUT checking record.status. Crash-window terminal-as-in_flight defect present.

votes.reasoning: Read index.py:135-164 (find_in_flight_runs appends the loaded record WITHOUT checking record.status), journal.py mark_run/upsert (write run file, release lock, THEN _refresh_index_entry) and _refresh_index_entry:641-693 (bumps single entry + index mtime). Reproduced in the project venv: wrote A terminal on disk without the index refresh (crash sim), refreshed via B's write, then _index_is_stale returned False and find_in_flight_runs returned ['A','B'] while load_run(A).status=='complete'. Terminal run permanently reads as in-flight.

title: Detached-worker lease records a bare pid with no host/boot identity, so on an NFS-shared journal home a live worker on another login node reads as a reclaimable dead lease (duplicate concurrent submit workers for the same run), and pid reuse after a reboot reads as a live holder (permanent DetachedLeaseHeld wedge the error text wrongly claims self-heals)
failure_scenario: The lease at ~/.claude/hpc/_detached/<block>-<run_id>.lease.json stamps only {run_id, block, pid, log_path, argv} (detached.py:426-437); liveness is psutil.pid_exists on the READING host (:272-295). Real HPC pattern: the CLI is driven from cluster login nodes with $HOME on NFS shared across login1/login2. (a) A live submit-s3 worker on login1 reads as a dead pid from login2 → the lease 'self-heals' and a second detached worker spawns for the same (run_id, block); two submit pipelines run concurrently against the same run — the advisory flock serialization (.submit_lock, filelock/fcntl) is itself unreliable cross-host on NFSv3, so both can qsub, producing duplicate arrays writing the same result dirs. (b) After a machine crash/reboot mid-campaign (the lease file survives, the worker doesn't), the recorded pid is frequently reused by an unrelated process → _guard_single_lease raises DetachedLeaseHeld naming a stranger's pid, and the error text's promise 'if the worker has died, its dead pid makes the lease stale and relaunching heals it' (:331-333) never comes true — the user is wedged until they hand-delete the lease file the message doesn't name a removal procedure for.
fix_sketch: Stamp hostname (socket.gethostname()) and the process start-time (psutil.Process(pid).create_time()) into the lease at detached.py:426-437. In _guard_single_lease: same-host + pid alive + matching create_time → live; same-host + pid alive but create_time mismatch → stale (pid reuse) → reclaim; different host → refuse-with-context rather than reclaim (cannot verify liveness remotely), or at minimum surface 'lease held by host X' so the user knows why. Mirror the check in _read_lease_holder_pid's refusal text.
evidence: Read _spawn_detached, _guard_single_lease, _pid_alive, and launch_submit_block_detached (detached_dir = _current_homedir()/'_detached' — the NFS-shared journal home). Lease JSON provably carries no host/boot identity. tests grep: test_supersession.py/test_resolve_submit_inputs.py only test same-process live-pid and pid<=0 dead cases; no test covers pid reuse or cross-host. The 2026-07-11 sweep and fix train touched neither this file's lease identity nor its liveness predicate.
triage: Verified detached.py:426-437 lease JSON stamps only {run_id,block,pid,log_path,argv} — no hostname/create_time. Cross-host/pid-reuse identity hole present.

votes.reasoning: Read detached.py: the lease JSON stamps only {run_id,block,pid,log_path,argv} (:426-437) with no hostname/create_time; liveness is _pid_alive->psutil.pid_exists on the READING host (:272-295), and _guard_single_lease reclaims any pid that isn't alive locally. detached_dir is the NFS-shared _current_homedir()/_detached (:522,528). So a re-launch from a second login node on shared $HOME reads a live remote worker's pid as dead and spawns a duplicate submit worker (the guard that exists to prevent this is host-blind), and a reused pid after reboot raises DetachedLeaseHeld naming a stranger despite the error text promising self-heal. The 'frequently reused pid' framing is mildly overstated, but the cross-host reclaim is deterministic under the stated NFS multi-login-node pattern.

title: Bug-sweep finding #25 (SubmitFlowSpec missing sge+mpi pe_name and mpi-requires-total_tasks=1 guards) is listed as retired in the upstream-fixes ledger but the fix never landed: SGE MPI specs still submit with no -pe (N ranks oversubscribing one core) and mpi+total_tasks>1 still quietly takes the array path
failure_scenario: Exactly the sweep's #25 scenario, still live at d731b12: an agent/human authors submit_flow.input.json (the documented public entry) with backend='sge', resources.mpi={ranks:16,launcher:'mpirun'}, no pe_name. SubmitFlowSpec validates it — its only mpi validator is still ranks_per_node divisibility (submit_flow.py wire:89-107); the model's validator list (540-577) has canary_only, supersedes, and pass_env_keys checks only. _engine.py:334-340 still silently emits NO -pe when pe_name is absent (its comment still asserts 'the wire guard (build_submit_spec) guarantees pe_name'), so qsub lands 16 ranks on one slot. Likewise resources.mpi with total_tasks>1 passes the wire and ops/submit_flow.py:2430-2438 only computes single_mpi_job=False and takes the array path — every array element runs task 0 and clobbers output (per the sweep's own analysis of the mpi template). The ledger (upstream-fixes-2026-07.md:502, 'Fired symptoms retired: ... #25') claims this was built in the eb89fc87..6d6deac1 train, but `git log eb89fc87..6d6deac1 -- src/hpc_agent/_wire/workflows/submit_flow.py` is empty and no commit in the train mentions pe_name/mpi; pe_name guards exist only on BuildSubmitSpecInput (_wire/actions/build_submit_spec.py:123-136).
fix_sketch: Land the sweep's own fix sketch: two model_validators on SubmitFlowSpec — (1) backend=='sge' and resources.mpi and not resources.mpi.pe_name -> raise the build_submit_spec 'sge with mpi requires pe_name' error; (2) resources.mpi and total_tasks>1 -> raise the 'mpi block requires total_tasks=1' error. Correct the ledger entry (or record the miss in its drift log per engineering-principles).
evidence: Read the full SubmitFlowSpec model at d731b12 (three validators, none mpi); read _engine.py 286-348 (silent no -pe branch unchanged); grepped all pe_name guard sites (only build_submit_spec.py); ran git log over the claimed fix train for the wire file (no commits) and grep for mpi/pe_name commits (none). tests/infra/backends/test_mpi.py still always supplies pe_name (per sweep note) — behavior unpinned.
triage: Rule-4 case: upstream-fixes:502 lists #25 retired, but verified SubmitFlowSpec (wire submit_flow.py:540-577) has only _canary_only/_supersedes/_no_empty_pass_env_keys validators — no sge+mpi/pe_name or total_tasks guard. Recorded fix did NOT land; finder is right.

votes.reasoning: SubmitFlowSpec carries only _canary_only/_supersedes/_no_empty_pass_env_keys validators; MpiSpec validates only ranks_per_node divisibility; _engine.py 338-340 silently emits no -pe when pe_name is absent. The pe_name guard exists solely on BuildSubmitSpecInput. A bare submit_flow.input.json with backend=sge + mpi + no pe_name validates and submits N ranks on one slot. Ledger upstream-fixes:502 lists #25 as retired but the fix never landed on this wire entry — Rule-4 confirmed. Primary skill path (S1/S2/S3) is guarded, so this bites the direct-primitive surface (medium).

title: Canary decision is re-evaluated after the rsync with a time-dependent input (#249 TTL cache), so a cache entry expiring during a long stage flips skip->run post-rsync and fires a canary whose sidecar never shipped — guaranteed sidecar_not_found, and with afterok the whole main sweep is dropped
failure_scenario: The pre-rsync mirror gate calls _should_run_canary(specs[i]) (2949); _submit_one_spec later re-runs _canary_decision(spec) (2447) AFTER the pre-stage smoke (up to 60s/executor), rsync (hours for a big stage — run #11's was 8.4 GB), deploy and uv probe. Both consult is_canary_validated_fresh(), which compares wall-clock now against the recorded validated_at (canary_cache.py:138-140, default TTL 4h). A cmd_sha validated ~4h ago reads FRESH pre-rsync (skip -> canary sidecar NOT mirrored, so it is not in the tree the rsync ships) and EXPIRED post-rsync (run -> _fire_canary mirrors the sidecar only LOCALLY at 2266, which the code's own comment at 2940-2942 says is 'too late to reach the cluster'). The canary task dies sidecar_not_found (#175 class); with enable_afterok_dependency every main wave is dropped by the scheduler; without it the campaign gets a spurious canary failure that verify-canary escalates. Worse, the main run WAS journal-recorded, so a retry now dedups (deduped=True) against a run whose arrays the scheduler dropped — a wedge needing manual reconcile. This is the residual of sweep finding #18 (whose raw-field half WAS fixed at 2949): the two decision sites still disagree because the input is time-varying, not because the predicate differs.
fix_sketch: Evaluate _canary_decision exactly once per spec inside _submit_flow_batch_locked, before the mirror loop, and thread the (run_canary, skip_reason) pair into _submit_one_spec (e.g. a per-spec decisions list or a field on a small per-spec context), so the pre-rsync mirror and the post-rsync canary leg act on one decision. Alternatively, in _submit_one_spec, when the pre-rsync decision was 'skip' and the post-rsync decision flips to 'run', honour the recorded pre-rsync decision.
evidence: Read both decision sites and _mirror_canary_sidecar call ordering (mirror loop 2926-2950 runs before _run_shared_prelude at 2983; _fire_canary's mirror at 2266 runs after); read canary_cache.is_canary_validated_fresh (pure wall-clock). tests/ops/test_submit_flow_canary_skip.py pins the decision function itself, not decision stability across the prelude; bug-sweep #18 covered only the raw-field gate, fixed at 2949.
triage: Verified second canary decision site _canary_decision(spec) at submit_flow.py:2447 (post-rsync) distinct from pre-rsync gate; both consult wall-clock TTL cache. Time-varying skip->run flip defect present.

votes.reasoning: The pre-rsync gate (_should_run_canary, 2949) and the post-rsync decision (_canary_decision, 2447) both consult is_canary_validated_fresh (wall-clock TTL, canary_cache 138-140); time only advances, so the sole possible flip is skip(fresh)->run(expired), the exact harmful direction. The post-rsync _fire_canary mirror (2266) is local-only and cannot reach the already-rsynced cluster tree, so the fired canary dies sidecar_not_found. Base failure (spurious canary) needs no opt-in; the main-sweep-drop worst case additionally needs opt-in afterok. Reachable given a near-TTL-boundary cmd_sha cache entry and a multi-hour stage.

title: _Engine.run's failure-path _discard ignores the inflight veto: one failed/timed-out command closes the shared per-host connection under its PEERS, severing their in-flight commands mid-command — the no-mid-command-sever rule is enforced against the sweeper but not against sibling commands
failure_scenario: Engine on; reconcile's poll tick fans THREE concurrent ssh commands to the same target over one ThreadPoolExecutor (reconcile.py:801-825: status report + combined-waves listing + alive check) — all multiplexed as channels on the single _HostConn (_MAX_SESSIONS=8). NFS lag makes the status-report leg hit its per-command timeout; _Engine.run's except branch calls self._discard(host, hc) unconditionally (ssh_engine.py:590) even though hc.inflight > 1. _do_close aborts the connection on the loop, so the two healthy peer commands die with ConnectionLost mid-command, each raises EngineUnavailable, and each silently re-executes one-shot (three cold handshakes against a possibly rate-limiting login node — the exact burst the slot/breaker machinery exists to avoid). Every monitor tick that hits one slow leg pays this cascade for the whole multi-day watch; submit_preflight/aggregate_preflight/status_preflight fanouts (submit_preflight.py:325, aggregate_preflight.py:248, status_preflight.py:183) hit the same path. A per-command timeout on a HEALTHY connection (asyncssh closes only the channel) did not even require the discard.
fix_sketch: In _Engine.run's failure handling, honor the same veto the sweeper honors: under _guard, if hc.inflight > 1 (peers active), mark the connection as draining (no new claims; e.g. hc.alive=False without closing) and let the LAST finisher perform the close, or discard only on evidence the CONNECTION is dead (ConnectionLost/channel-open failure) while a bare per-command asyncssh TimeoutError keeps the connection and only abandons the channel. Add a test: two stub commands in flight, fail one, assert the second completes over the engine without EngineUnavailable.
evidence: Read _Engine.run/_discard/_sweep_idle at d731b12: the sweeper's stale predicate has the 'inflight == 0' veto (ssh_engine.py:707-711, test-pinned) but the failure-path discard at :590 and the backstop discard at :581 have no inflight check, and _discard (:716-726) closes unconditionally. Confirmed real same-target concurrency at reconcile.py:801-825. No test covers a discard with peers in flight (test list at tests/infra/test_ssh_engine.py:127-794 has only sweeper and single-command cases).
triage: Verified _Engine.run ssh_engine.py:581,590 call self._discard unconditionally with no inflight>1 veto (unlike the sweeper's veto). Peer-sever cascade present.

related.note: Compounding within the ssh engine: F56 (_discard closes the shared connection under in-flight peers) severs sibling commands mid-flight, which then each hit F55's post-dispatch EngineUnavailable -> silent one-shot re-execution. Distinct root causes (unconditional discard vs no already-dispatched distinction).

votes.reasoning: reconcile.py:801-825 fans 3 concurrent same-target ssh commands over ThreadPoolExecutor, all multiplexed on one per-host _HostConn (inflight up to 3). _Engine.run's failure discards at ssh_engine.py:581/590 call self._discard unconditionally with no inflight>1 veto, unlike the sweeper's inflight==0 veto (711); _discard->_do_close->conn.close() aborts the whole connection, severing peers mid-command into EngineUnavailable and one-shot re-exec. Real asymmetry; engine-opt-in gated, medium fits.

title: The rsync-less content-hash delta push silently IGNORES internal-slash exclude patterns: files a caller excluded with an anchored pattern (e.g. rsync_excludes=['data/interim/']) are excluded by the rsync and full-copy tar modes but SHIP in delta mode — the same push command transfers different file sets depending on whether the remote manifest round-trip succeeded
failure_scenario: Windows control plane (no rsync — the repo's supported live path per run-#11/#12). Spec carries rsync_excludes=['data/interim/'] to keep a multi-GB intermediate tree off the cluster. First push has no remote manifest -> full-copy tar path, which DOES emit --exclude=data/interim (transport.py:1117-1123) — the tree stays local. Every subsequent delete=True push takes the delta path: _pushable_relpaths uses _path_excluded, whose docstring says an internal-slash pattern 'matches no single component and is therefore inert' (transport.py:359-362) — so the local manifest INCLUDES data/interim/**, the remote manifest (same inert semantics in _REMOTE_MANIFEST_SNIPPET) lacks it, manifest_delta marks it all 'missing', and the delta tar ships the entire excluded tree over the slow VPN onto the shared cluster filesystem. The disclosure line just counts it as 'changed/new' — nothing says an exclude was dropped. Multi-GB wasted transfer per push, and content the user deliberately kept off a shared system now lives there (the bare-name MANDATORY guard for clusters.yaml survives, but any user secret excluded via an anchored path pattern does not).
fix_sketch: Give _path_excluded (and the remote snippet, which must stay in lockstep) real internal-slash semantics: a pattern containing '/' matches when the pattern's component sequence prefixes/anchors the relpath (mirroring rsync's transfer-root anchoring, same shape _remote_clean_cmd already implements via find -path at transport.py:241-244). Alternatively, refuse the delta and fall back to the disclosed full copy when any caller exclude contains an internal slash. Add a test: rsync-less delta push with exclude=['a/b/'] must not place a/b/** in to_ship while the tar full-copy run of the same spec excludes it.
evidence: Read _path_excluded/_pushable_relpaths/_REMOTE_MANIFEST_SNIPPET and both tar exclude paths at d731b12. The inertness is acknowledged only as keeping the two MANIFESTS consistent with the cosmetic disclosure walk — but in delta mode the same predicate decides what SHIPS (transport.py:1472-1499), where it silently diverges from the rsync/tar semantics the 'exclude' contract documents. rsync_excludes is an agent/spec-authored list (submit_flow.py:409/791) where anchored dir patterns are natural. No test in tests/infra/test_remote_rsync_fallback.py exercises an internal-slash exclude in delta mode. Not in the sweep (#36 covered runtime-file anomalies) or the architecture review.
triage: Verified _path_excluded transport.py:359-372 treats internal-slash patterns as inert; delta-mode _pushable_relpaths uses it, so anchored dir excludes ship in delta mode while tar/rsync honor them. Divergence present.

votes.reasoning: _path_excluded (347-372) makes internal-slash patterns inert, and delta-mode _pushable_relpaths (584-602) uses it, so data/interim/** enters to_ship (1472-1499). I empirically confirmed GNU tar 1.35 --exclude=data/interim excludes the subtree, so the full-copy tar path (1117-1123) honors it while the delta path ships it — the same push command diverges on whether the manifest round-trip succeeded. Reachable on rsync-less (Windows) plane with a caller anchored-dir exclude; default excludes are bare names and unaffected. Medium holds.

title: rsync_push rewrites the push manifest unconditionally after a delta transfer, so one failed or cap-refused prune permanently downgrades those remote extras from 'manifest-known (prunable)' to 'ANOMALY (never touched)' — and the tool's own printed remediation ('raise the cap and re-push') can never work
failure_scenario: Rsync-less campaign: push N shipped module old_name.py; user renames it locally. Push N+1's prune plan correctly finds old_name.py manifest-known — but the plan is REFUSED over the twin cap (or _execute_prune fails on a transient ssh error, which is fail-open and its False return ignored, transport.py:953-958). The WARN tells the user to 'raise the cap (HPC_DEPLOY_PRUNE_MAX_FILES / HPC_DEPLOY_PRUNE_MAX_BYTES)' and re-push (transport.py:813-817). But rsync_push then unconditionally overwrites .hpc/.push_manifest.json with ONLY the current local paths (transport.py:1522-1527), erasing the record that old_name.py was ours. On the re-push with the raised cap, _read_prior_push_manifest no longer contains it -> plan_prune classifies it an ANOMALY -> never deleted, plus a 'needs a human decision' WARN on every subsequent push for the rest of the campaign. Meanwhile the stale old_name.py stays importable on the cluster (PEP 420 tree on PYTHONPATH), so cluster-side 'import old_name' keeps silently succeeding against dead code — masking the rename for every array task.
fix_sketch: Write the push manifest as the UNION of the current local paths and any manifest-known extras that were not successfully pruned this push (plan.refused, plan.to_prune minus what _execute_prune confirmed, and the untouched prunable set on exception) — i.e. keep provenance for everything we still believe is ours on the remote. _execute_prune already returns a success bool that _prune_manifest_known_extras currently discards (transport.py:953-958); thread it (and the pruned path list) back to rsync_push's manifest write.
evidence: Read the full delta-push tail at d731b12: _prune_manifest_known_extras journals a refusal and returns (transport.py:924-935), _execute_prune's return value is unused, and _write_push_manifest runs unconditionally after every successful transfer with paths=[local entries only] (transport.py:1522-1527). tests/infra/test_transport_prune.py (test_over_bound_refuses_with_disclosure etc.) pins the refusal itself but never the NEXT push's manifest contents after a refusal/failed delete — the provenance loss is untested. The ruling-6 design note (transport.py:691-714) claims the manifest is 'the record of what THIS control plane last shipped', which the overwrite falsifies for still-remote prior shipments.
triage: Verified transport.py:1522-1527 _write_push_manifest runs unconditionally with paths=[local entries only] after a delta push, regardless of prune refusal/failure. Provenance-loss defect present.

votes.reasoning: _prune_manifest_known_extras returns without deleting on plan.refused (924-935) or swallows _execute_prune failure (959), then rsync_push unconditionally calls _write_push_manifest with local-only paths (1522-1527) after any successful transfer, erasing the provenance of a still-remote prior shipment. The next push's _read_prior_push_manifest lacks it, so plan_prune reclassifies it as an ANOMALY (never pruned, WARN each push), defeating the printed 'raise the cap and re-push' remediation (813-817). Reachable via cap-refusal or a transient prune-ssh error. Medium holds.

title: gpu_preamble.sh unconditionally clobbers PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF, ignoring the preamble's own HPC_-override convention — user job_env values are silently overwritten on every GPU task
failure_scenario: A user whose model needs `PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=expandable_segments:True` (a common fix for fragmentation OOMs on long trainings) sets it in the spec's job_env; the scheduler exports it into the job, then gpu_preamble.sh line 34 unconditionally re-exports `max_split_size_mb:128` AFTER the injected env, so every GPU task runs with the framework's allocator config instead of the user's. Tasks OOM on the cluster but not in local runs; nothing in the logs names the override (unlike every other knob in these preambles — CUBLAS_WORKSPACE_CONFIG, XLA_FLAGS, thread caps, locale — which all honor the `HPC_<NAME>` unset/empty override convention documented in the same file).
fix_sketch: Apply the file's own convention: `if [ "${HPC_PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF-max_split_size_mb:128}" != "" ]; then export PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF="${HPC_PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF:-max_split_size_mb:128}"; fi` — or at minimum `export PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF="${PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF:-max_split_size_mb:128}"` so an injected job_env value survives.
evidence: Read gpu_preamble.sh in full: lines 47-61 document and implement the HPC_-override convention for CUBLAS/XLA; line 34 predates it and takes no override. Template headers confirm job_env is injected before the preambles are sourced (qsub -v / sbatch --export), so the clobber wins. No test covers PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF; not mentioned in the sweep, fix plan, or architecture review.

title: An empty cluster entry in clusters.yaml (`mycluster:` with no body) crashes check-preflight with an internal AttributeError envelope (exit 3) instead of a failed check, poisoning every preflight-running composite

title: install_agent_assets never prunes assets removed or renamed by a package upgrade — stale skills/commands/agent definitions and their Skill(...) allow rules persist in ~/.claude and keep getting invoked against removed CLI verbs
where: src/hpc_agent/agent_assets.py:907-986, src/hpc_agent/agent_assets.py:725-836 (contrast: deny rules DO self-heal)
failure_scenario: A user upgrades hpc-agent mid-project (`pip install -U`). install-commands (re-run automatically at the top of every submit_preflight) copies the current commands/skills/agents over ~/.claude but only ever adds/overwrites — _install_tree has no removal step (the only unlink is the 0-byte collision heal). Anything a release deleted or renamed stays installed: e.g. the §6 worker removal dropped the haiku-pinned hpc-worker agent definition (agent_assets.py:966-970 says core now ships none), but a pre-§6 install keeps ~/.claude/agents/hpc-worker.md forever, and _merge_skill_permissions keeps every old Skill(<name>) allow rule. Notably the codebase already self-heals the OTHER asset classes (stale hooks are 'updated', stale blanket deny rules are 'removed'), so the gap is specifically skills/commands/agents.

On the next install, delete previously-owned names absent from the current tree (and drop their Skill(...) allow rules via the same merge machinery), reporting them under a `removed` key like _merge_deny_rules does. Never touch names the manifest doesn't own (user's own skills stay safe).
evidence: Read _install_tree (copy-only; commands/skills/agents loops have no deletion), install_agent_assets (core then plugin overlay, still copy-only), _resolve_dir_collision (unlinks only 0-byte files), _merge_skill_permissions (add-only), and the explicit self-heal in _merge_deny_rules ('a stale blanket rule is still removed — the upgrade self-heals') plus _merge_hook_entry's 'updated' path — demonstrating removal was designed for two asset classes and omitted for the three copied trees. The §6 comment at agent_assets.py:966-970 documents a shipped-then-removed agent definition as a concrete stale artifact. No test covers upgrade-with-removed-asset.
triage: install_agent_assets copy-only tree install never prunes removed/renamed skills/commands/agents while deny/hook classes self-heal. Real upgrade dead-end. Not banked.

title: SGE suspended states (s/S/T — routine under subordinate-queue preemption) classify as alive and map to RUNNING, so a preempted task shows as progressing indefinitely
failure_scenario: On an SGE/UGE cluster with subordinate queues (common preemption mechanism, incl. UGE campus clusters), a running array task gets suspended: qstat state 's'/'S' (or threshold 'T'). classify_scheduler_state's sge branch checks only 'E' (error) and 'h' (held); suspended tokens fall through to 'alive', and _alive_task_status ('r' not in 's', 'w' not in 's' -> RUNNING) reports RUNNING. A user watching /status during a multi-day campaign sees tasks 'running' that have made no progress for hours, with no held/stalled signal — the SLURM branch deliberately buckets SUSPENDED/STOPPED as held (:871) for exactly this reason, so the families disagree on identical semantics.

evidence: Read _engine.py:884-890 (sge classify: only 'E' and 'h' checked) and :945-950 (fallthrough to RUNNING), vs the slurm branch's SUSPENDED->held handling at :869-872 with its slurm-drmaa citation — the same drmaa mapping treats SGE suspended as held. tests/infra/test_scheduler_states.py exercises sge tokens but no suspended case (grepped). Untested, unmentioned in the fixed/refuted lists.
triage: SGE classify branch handles only E/h; suspended s/S/T fall through to alive->RUNNING while SLURM buckets SUSPENDED as held. Real status-misparse. Not in fixed/refuted.

votes.reasoning: classify_scheduler_state's sge branch (_engine.py:884-890) special-cases only 'E' and 'h', so suspend tokens s/S/T fall through to 'alive', and _alive_task_status (947-950) maps them to RUNNING (for 's': 'r' not in s True, 'w' in s False -> RUNNING), whereas the slurm branch buckets SUSPENDED/STOPPED as held (871). On a preemption/subordinate-queue SGE cluster a suspended task shows RUNNING indefinitely. Reachable but observability-only (settling is unaffected since suspended is correctly still-alive), so low is appropriate.

title: prune_old_runs evicts run sidecars purely by count/mtime — including runs that are still in_flight or parked on a verdict — and canary mirror sidecars halve the effective cap, so a long campaign silently loses live runs' config (result_dir_template, trial_params, wave_map, cluster) mid-flight

title: update_run_sidecar_job_ids' locking comment claims serialization 'against concurrent write_run_sidecar callers', but write_run_sidecar takes no lock at all — a Step-6d sidecar rewrite racing the post-qsub finalize can erase job_ids, flipping a live run to orphan (prune-eligible, dedup-invisible)
failure_scenario: update_run_sidecar_job_ids routes through atomic_locked_update on <run_id>.json.lock with an explicit comment that this 'serializes against concurrent write_run_sidecar callers' (runs.py:1041-1044). But write_run_sidecar itself writes via bare atomic_write_json with NO flock (runs.py:473-478), so the claimed exclusion only holds between two locked-RMW callers. Two surfaces on one run_id — e.g. an agent re-running resolve-submit-inputs / the write-run-sidecar CLI (ops/write_run_sidecar.py, unguarded by the submit batch lock) while a detached S2/S3 worker sits between qsub and its finalize — can interleave so the fresh jobless sidecar lands AFTER the job_ids stamp. The run then reads as orphan (is_orphan_sidecar, runs.py:921-963): prune_orphan_sidecars deletes it after 5 minutes, find_run_by_cmd_sha(skip_orphans=True) stops seeing it, and the cluster jobs it actually landed become untracked — the exact orphaned-jobs class the finalize exists to prevent.

At minimum correct the false comment at runs.py:1041-1044 per the repo's verify-a-guard-can-fire rule.
evidence: Read both functions on d731b12: write_run_sidecar → local _atomic_write_json forwarder → infra.io.atomic_write_json (mkstemp+replace, no advisory_flock anywhere in the chain); update_run_sidecar_job_ids → atomic_locked_update (flock held only for its own RMW). The comment's claim is unimplementable as written. No test exercises a concurrent write_run_sidecar vs finalize interleaving. The G12 fix train addressed atomicity (torn files), not this lost-update/lock-asymmetry, and neither the refuted list nor the architecture review covers it.
triage: Verified write_run_sidecar runs.py:473-478 writes via _atomic_write_json with no advisory flock, so the 'serializes against concurrent write_run_sidecar' comment is false. Lock asymmetry present.

votes.reasoning: Read write_run_sidecar:473-479 -> _atomic_write_json:483 -> infra.io.atomic_write_json:110 (mkstemp+fsync+replace, NO advisory_flock anywhere in the chain), versus update_run_sidecar_job_ids:1041-1064 whose comment claims atomic_locked_update 'serializes against concurrent write_run_sidecar callers'. Since write_run_sidecar takes no lock, that exclusion is unimplementable — the comment is false, and a jobless write racing the post-qsub job_ids stamp can land last and erase job_ids, flipping a live run to orphan. title: The journal-existence guards in index.py can never fire — journal_dir() mkdirs + writes repo.json as a side effect of being called — so every read path (doctor from the wrong cwd, journal polls, status queries) silently mints stray ~/.claude/hpc/<hash>/ namespaces that then pollute the fleet=True walks

The attention-queue module already states and implements exactly this posture (attention_queue.py:1501-1502) — lift its idiom into state/ so readers and writers stop sharing the dir-creating accessor.
evidence: Read journal_dir (unconditional mkdir + repo.json write), JournalLayout.root/.runs (both create), and the three index.py guard sites; the guard expression is self-defeating by construction. attention_queue.py:1501-1502's comment confirms the team knows journal_dir scaffolds on read and worked around it locally, but the state-layer readers were never given the non-creating probe. No test asserts a read leaves the journal home untouched. Not covered by the 2026-07 fix train, the refuted list, or the banked architecture proposals.

Different mechanisms/fixes, same accessor.

title: Canary replay branch reuses a stale scheduler job id as the afterok gate: after a crash-then-later retry, the main array is submitted with --dependency=afterok:<purged job>, which SLURM rejects — and the forever-in_flight canary journal record makes every retry repeat it
failure_scenario: Attempt 1 (enable_afterok_dependency=true) fires the canary, records it (journal in_flight), then dies before the main array lands (network drop / laptop sleep — the exact window the replay branch documents at 2452-2456). Nobody monitors the orphan canary, so its journal record stays in_flight even after the 1-task job finishes and slurmctld purges it (MinJobAge, default ~300s). The user retries the next morning: replay branch reuses existing_canary.job_ids (2462), gate_job_ids (2505-2511) builds --dependency=afterok:<purged id> --kill-on-invalid-dep=yes (_engine.py:179-187), and sbatch rejects the submission ('Job dependency problem') -> RemoteCommandFailed. Nothing ever refreshes the canary record (in_flight is not resubmittable-terminal, journal.py is_resubmittable_terminal), so EVERY retry reuses the same dead id: the run is wedged until the user hand-edits the spec (canary=false / enable_afterok_dependency=false) or manually reconciles the canary record — none of which the error message suggests. PBS variant: -W depend=afterok on an unknown id is likewise rejected or held forever.
fix_sketch: Only gate on canary_job_ids when the canary was fired THIS call (the fresh branch), or, on replay, verify the reused ids are still scheduler-visible (a cheap squeue/qstat probe through the backend) before building the afterok flag — falling back to the un-gated co-submission (the documented SGE posture) when the canary is already terminal/purged. Additionally, a replayed canary whose journal record is in_flight but whose job has vanished should be reconciled rather than reused.
evidence: Read the replay branch (reuses recorded ids for any non-resubmittable-terminal record, i.e. in_flight or complete), the gate construction, and _build_afterok_dependency_flag for slurm/pbs. SLURM rejects dependencies naming jobs absent from slurmctld at submit time (domain knowledge; MinJobAge default 300s). Grepped tests: tests/ops/submit/test_canary_gate.py exercises the gate with same-call canaries only; no test replays a stale canary into the afterok path. Sweep docs mention afterok only inside finding #18's text.
triage: Verified replay branch submit_flow.py:2450-2463 reuses existing_canary.job_ids for any non-resubmittable-terminal (in_flight/complete) record. Stale afterok-gate defect present.

votes.reasoning: The code defect is real: the replay branch (2451-2463) reuses in_flight canary job_ids (in_flight is not resubmittable-terminal, journal 603), builds an afterok gate from them (_engine 179-187), and the in_flight canary is never reconciled so retries repeat. But the wedge requires ALL of: enable_afterok_dependency (defaults False, opt-in), a crash in the canary-record->main-land window, an unmonitored orphan, a MinJobAge purge, a naive re-submit, AND adverse scheduler behavior on a purged-job afterok (config/version dependent). That stacked, opt-in-gated, timing-specific chain is far harder to reach than presented. What survives: stale-in_flight-canary reuse into afterok with no visibility check or reconciliation.

title: PBS job_id_regex has no phrase/line anchor — any decimal or version string in a qsub banner ('est. wait 1.5 hours', 'PBS Pro 2022.1') matches before the real id line, so the run is journaled under a phantom job id
failure_scenario: Many PBS sites wrap qsub or configure submit filters that print informational lines to stdout before the job id. submit_one does JOB_ID_REGEX.search(stdout) over the whole buffer; the pbspro/torque pattern `(\d+)(?:\[\d*\])?\.[A-Za-z0-9_.-]+` matches '1.5' in 'estimated start in 1.5 hours' (group(1)='1') or '2022.1' in a version banner before ever reaching '12345[].pbs01' on the last line. The run record then tracks job '1': liveness/state queries poll a nonexistent (or worse, someone else's ancient) id, kill issues `qdel 1`, and the real array runs untracked — an orphan the monitor can never see. SLURM/SGE profiles explicitly anchor on 'Submitted batch job'/'Your job' for exactly this banner class (their own comments, profile.py:276-278,301-303); the PBS profiles got only a shape anchor any dotted number satisfies.
fix_sketch: Anchor the PBS patterns to a line that IS a job id: `(?m)^(\d+)(?:\[\d*\])?\.[A-Za-z0-9_.-]+\s*$` — qsub prints the id alone on its own line on both forks — optionally preferring the last such match. Pure profile-data change (job_id_regex string), no engine change; add a poisoned-banner case to tests/infra/backends/test_pbs.py alongside the existing happy-path asserts at lines 38-39.
evidence: Read profile.py: SLURM (:276-278) and SGE (:301-303) comments document the banner-poisoning class and anchor on phrases; PBSPRO/TORQUE (:322,:333) use the bare dotted-number shape with a comment claiming the .server suffix is the anchor — but '1.5', '2022.1', '10.0.0.1' all satisfy `\d+\.[A-Za-z0-9_.-]+`. __init__.py:570-573 uses .search over full stdout and returns the FIRST match. tests/infra/backends/test_pbs.py:38-39 tests only clean single-token stdout; no banner case exists. Not in the fixed/refuted lists.
triage: Confirmed PBS regex has no phrase/line anchor (SLURM:278/SGE:303 do); '1.5'/'2022.1' in a qsub banner match via .search before the real id line. Distinct from F36. Not in fixed/refuted.

related.note: Two distinct defects in the same PBS job_id_regex/capture (profile.py:322,333 + __init__.py:570-573): F36 drops the array '[]' marker; F39 lacks a line anchor so a qsub banner decimal is captured. Same code, different mechanisms — likely fixed together.

votes.reasoning: The regex mechanism is real: profile.py:322,333 use an unanchored (\d+)(?:\[\d*\])?\.[A-Za-z0-9_.-]+, __init__.py:570-573 does .search over full stdout and returns the FIRST match, and '1.5'/'2022.1' do match with group(1)='1'/'2022' — while SLURM (:278) and SGE (:303) deliberately phrase-anchor. But standard PBS qsub writes only the job id to stdout; the concrete poisoning requires a non-standard leading dotted-number banner on stdout that the finding asserts ('many sites wrap qsub') without any repo or scheduler-doc evidence. Genuine defense-in-depth inconsistency, but the failing state is not clearly reachable in normal PBS usage.
